4.  **Use Standard, Professional Formatting:** Custom has standardized letter format; startling variations indicate freakishness rather than originality and undermine professional trust.
5.  **Master One Subject Per Communication:** A business letter should handle only one subject; covering two subjects requires two separate letters to enable proper filing and retrieval.

### Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-era Advice | Modern Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| "Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo." | Use clean, simple email templates or word processing defaults. Overly decorative fonts, colored backgrounds, or excessive formatting in emails is unprofessional. |
| "The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around." | Emails and documents should have consistent spacing, clear paragraph breaks, and sufficient white space for easy reading on screen. |
| "A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered." | Send separate emails for distinct topics. Use clear subject lines so each message can be filed, searched, and acted upon independently. |
| "Practically all business letters are typewritten... it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up." | Most business writing is typed electronically. The writer must understand formatting (headings, bullet points, font choices) to oversee final presentation. |
| "Special care should be taken with the outside address... 'City' is not sufficient and should never be used." | In email, the "To" field and subject line must be precise. Avoid vague recipients (e.g., "Team" without context) and ensure the correct address is entered. |

### Dated Advice – Update Without Losing the Principle

**Dated Advice:** The recommendation that stenographers (young girls) handle typing and that the dictator must check for "pleasing make-up" is now obsolete. Most professionals do their own typing, and gender-specific roles are inappropriate.

**Underlying Principle:** The final output of a written communication must be visually clean and professionally formatted.

**Modern Update:** Every business writer is responsible for the visual presentation of their own work. Instead of checking a typist, you should apply the same attention to detail: ensure consistent fonts, proper line breaks, and correct use of formatting tools (like bullet points or bold text) to create a clear, scannable document.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
Mary Lennox, a sickly, sour-looking child, is sent to Misselthwaite Manor after her parents die in a cholera outbreak in India. Born to a neglectful mother and busy father, Mary was raised by servants who obeyed her every whim, making her selfish and tyrannical. When cholera strikes, her ayah dies, and servants flee in panic. Mary hides in the nursery, forgotten by everyone. She eats leftover food and drinks wine, becoming drowsy. The outbreak kills her parents, leaving Mary orphaned and alone. She is eventually discovered and sent to England to live with her uncle, beginning her lonely journey at Misselthwaite Manor.

### Mary’s Character Traits with Examples
- **Disagreeable and sour**: She has “a sour expression” and is described as “the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen.”
- **Selfish and tyrannical**: At six, she is “as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived,” because servants always obeyed her.
- **Angry and violent**: When a strange servant replaces her ayah, Mary “threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her.”
- **Fretful and cross**: She awakens “feeling very cross” and mutters insults like “Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” under a tree.
- **Neglected and isolated**: During the cholera chaos, she is “forgotten by everyone” and hides alone in the nursery.

### Setting and Emotional Isolation
The story opens in India during a deadly cholera outbreak, creating immediate emotional distance. Mary’s bungalow becomes a place of panic and abandonment—servants flee or die, her parents ignore her, and she hides unnoticed in the nursery. The “confusion and bewilderment” of the second day leaves Mary “forgotten by everyone,” emphasizing her isolation. The setting’s heat, disease, and chaos physically separate her from care, while the empty dining room and hurriedly abandoned meal underline how utterly alone she is. This environment mirrors Mary’s internal loneliness, cutting her off from warmth or connection.

### Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary became so selfish and angry? How might her parents and the servants have contributed to her behavior?
2. How does Mary react when the strange servant says her ayah cannot come? What does this tell you about how she treats people?
3. During the cholera outbreak, Mary is “forgotten by everyone.” How do you think that made her feel? Have you ever felt forgotten or left out?

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Concise 100-Word Overview
The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water on Earth, which is stored in oceans, atmosphere, land surfaces, and underground. Oceans hold 96% of all water (saline), while fresh water exists in lakes, rivers, ice, and groundwater. Water changes form (liquid, solid, gas) and moves through evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. Solar energy and gravity drive these processes. Humans use water and alter land, affecting movement and availability. Understanding these impacts helps promote sustainable water use.

### Concept Map (Text Form)
Sun’s energy → Evaporation/Evapotranspiration → Atmosphere (water vapor)  
Atmosphere → Precipitation (rain, snow, hail) → Land/Oceans  
Land → Snowmelt/Runoff/Streamflow → Oceans  
Land → Infiltration → Groundwater (aquifers)  
Groundwater → Natural discharge (springs, rivers, ocean) → Oceans/Oceans → Evaporation → Atmosphere  
Oceans ← Storage (96% saline water)  
Land ← Storage (fresh water: lakes, reservoirs, rivers, ice sheets, glaciers, snowpack)

### Key Terms (with Definitions)
1. **Water cycle** – The continuous movement and storage of water on Earth, including changes between liquid, solid, and gas.
2. **Evaporation** – The process by which liquid water turns into water vapor, driven by solar energy.
3. **Evapotranspiration** – The combined movement of water from land and oceans into the atmosphere through evaporation and plant transpiration.
4. **Precipitation** – Water falling from clouds as rain, snow, or hail, driven by gravity.
5. **Infiltration** – The movement of water from the land surface into the ground.
6. **Groundwater recharge** – The process by which infiltrated water replenishes underground aquifers.
7. **Runoff** – Water flowing across the land surface, typically toward streams, rivers, or oceans.
8. **Aquifer** – An underground layer that stores and transmits groundwater.

### Human Influence
Human actions affect water movement, storage, and availability through water use and land use changes. These activities alter how water cycles between pools, influencing the timing and speed of flows such as runoff, infiltration, and evaporation. Human water consumption reduces the amount available in certain pools, while land-use modifications (e.g., construction, agriculture) can change infiltration rates and surface runoff patterns. Understanding these impacts is essential for sustainable water management.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Update on Warehouse Operations and Customer Experience  

**Team,**  

I’m writing to give you a direct, unvarnished update on the situation we’ve been navigating this week.  

As many of you have felt firsthand—whether in the warehouse, on customer calls, or in the ripple effects on your daily work—our migration to a new warehouse management system has not gone as smoothly as we planned. I want to explain what happened, what we’re doing right now, and what you can expect next week.  

**What Happened**  

Last weekend, we completed the cutover from our legacy warehouse management system to a new platform. The transition involved reconfiguring how inventory is tracked, how orders are routed to picking stations, and how shipping labels are generated. During the first three days of go-live, we encountered two specific issues:  

1. **Inventory location data did not fully sync,** causing pickers to spend extra time searching for items that were marked as “in stock” but not properly mapped to their actual storage bins.  
2. **The label-printing module intermittently timed out** during high-volume periods, slowing pack-and-ship throughput by roughly 30% on Tuesday and Wednesday.  

These were not failures of effort. Our warehouse, IT, and operations teams worked around the clock to diagnose and patch each issue. However, the cumulative effect was a one-week delay in outbound shipping speed. Orders that normally leave our facility within 24 hours are now taking 48–72 hours to ship.  

**Current Customer Impact**  

- **Approximately 12% of last week’s orders are still in “processing” status** beyond our standard SLA.  
- **Customer support volume has increased by nearly 40%** since Monday, driven primarily by order-status inquiries and delivery-date concerns.  
- **First-contact resolution time has slipped**, because our support team is manually verifying shipment status in two systems while the legacy reporting tool remains read-only.  

I want to be clear: our customers are frustrated, and that frustration is landing on your shoulders. I see that. And I want you to know that leadership is not asking you to “absorb” this—we are taking active steps to reduce the burden.  

**What We Are Doing Today (Friday)**  

1. **Warehouse:** We have paused all non-critical inbound receiving to focus all picking and packing capacity on clearing the backlog. Every available associate is on the floor, and we’ve added a second shift for Saturday.  
2. **Customer Support:** We have activated our overflow call center partner and are routing Tier 1 order-status inquiries to an automated SMS update system so that agents can focus on complex cases.  
3. **Systems:** IT deployed a hotfix overnight that resolved the label-printing timeout. Since 6:00 AM today, throughput has returned to 95% of pre-migration levels. The inventory sync issue is still under investigation—we believe it is a data-mapping error that will require a full re-index, which is scheduled for Sunday night.  

**What Will Change Next Week**  

- By Monday morning, we expect the backlog to be reduced by 60–70%.  
- By Tuesday, all orders placed before this Sunday should be shipped or in transit.  
- We are implementing a **two-hour escalation SLA** for any warehouse system anomaly: if an issue is detected, the warehouse manager and an IT lead will run a diagnostic within two hours. No more waiting for “next-day” patches.  
- Starting Monday, customer support agents will have a dedicated **read-only dashboard** that shows real-time order status from the new system, eliminating the need to check two databases.  

**How to Escalate Urgent Issues**  

If you are facing a situation that is blocking your ability to serve a customer or complete a task:  

- **Warehouse associates:** Report directly to your shift supervisor, who will escalate to the Warehouse Operations Lead (contact in Slack #ops-urgent).  
- **Customer support agents:** Use the `/escalate-order` Slack slash command with the order ID and a brief description. This pushes the case to the priority queue monitored by our Ops team.  
- **All other teams:** If you see a pattern (e.g., repeated error codes, unusual return requests), send a single message to #it-ops-alerts with the subject line “Pattern: [brief description]”. Do not send individual support tickets for systemic issues—one alert triggers a group response.  

**Manager Talking Points (for your team meetings)**  

Please use these four points when your team asks about the situation:  

1. **“The migration caused two specific technical issues—inventory sync and label printing. Both have been identified, and the label fix is already live. The inventory fix is scheduled for Sunday night.”**  
2. **“Backlog is real but shrinking. We expect 60–70% cleared by Monday morning and all pre-Sunday orders shipped by Tuesday.”**  
3. **“If a customer is angry, you do not need to absorb that anger alone. Use the escalation path. We want to solve problems, not take all the stress.”**  
4. **“This was not a planning failure by any single team. The migration was tested, but we missed a data-mapping scenario. We are fixing it, and we are building better testing protocols for future changes.”**  

**Closing**  

I know the past week has been exhausting, especially for our warehouse and customer support teams. I cannot promise zero disruptions in the future—system migrations are imperfect—but I can promise that we will communicate clearly, fix problems fast, and stop asking you to compensate for system gaps.  

Thank you for holding the line this week.  

Best,  
[Your Name]  
COO

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
The Very Careful Snail and the Sparrow Who Forgot to Land

The summer rain had stopped, leaving the garden sparkling and fresh. Every leaf wore a tiny, shiny hat of water, and the air smelled like wet mud and happy earth.

Under a fat rhubarb leaf, a small snail named Silas was very busy. Silas was a mapmaker. Not a mapmaker of cities or roads, but a mapmaker of puddles. He carried a soft, rolled-up leaf in his pocket, and on it he had drawn every single puddle in the whole garden. There was the Giant Puddle under the dripping tap (very deep, very cold), the Tiny Mirror Puddle by the rose bush (shallow, perfect for a sip), and the Wiggly River Puddle that ran along the garden path after a good rain.

“One, two, three…” Silas murmured, touching each puddle on his map with his delicate little eyestalks. “The Shiny Puddle by the fence is getting smaller. It will be gone by lunchtime.” He made a tiny note in the corner. Knowing where the puddles would be was very important to a careful snail.

Suddenly, a brown blur whooshed past his head. WHAP! Silas ducked into his shell.

“Whoa there!” chirped a breathless voice.

Silas peeked out. A young sparrow was wobbling on a low branch, her feathers all ruffled and her eyes wide and bright. Her name was Pip.

“That was close,” said Silas quietly.

“Sorry!” said Pip, hopping from one foot to the other. “I’m practicing! I want to fly all the way to the top of the old oak tree! I almost made it!”

Silas looked up. The old oak tree was very, very tall. It seemed to tickle the clouds. “You are a brave flier,” he said.

“I’m not brave yet,” said Pip, puffing out her chest. “I’m fast. I’ll be brave when I touch the highest branch. I just have to get there as fast as I can!”

She launched herself into the air. For five beautiful seconds, she soared upwards, her wings beating like a tiny heart. But then she tried to land on a slippery sunflower stem. Her feet skidded. Her wings flapped wildly. With a little *thump*, she tumbled into the Wiggly River Puddle.

Splash!

Pip came up spluttering. “Oof. That landing thing is tricky.”

Silas slowly made his way over. “You forgot to slow down,” he said.

“Slowing down is boring!” chirped Pip, shaking water from her feathers. “Flying is the exciting part!”

Just then, a fat cloud drifted over the sun. A breeze stirred the wet leaves. And right in the middle of the Giant Puddle, a single, round leaf began to spin in the wind, like a little green boat.

Pip’s eyes got big. “What’s that? It’s magic!”

“It’s the wind,” said Silas. “The breeze is pushing the leaf where it wants it to go. See how the leaf doesn’t fight it? It just waits and lets the wind guide it.”

Pip stared. “But I want to go UP!” she said. “I need stronger wind.”

“The wind likes patience,” said Silas gently. “If you wait and watch, it will show you the way.”

Pip did not like waiting. But she was soaked, and a little bit sore, so she hopped next to Silas. They watched the leaf spin and drift. The sun came back out. The puddle began to shimmer.

“Look,” said Silas, pointing a slimy little antenna at the Shiny Puddle by the fence. “See how it’s smaller than before? The sun is drinking it up. By tomorrow, it will be gone. Puddles are like flying. They don’t last forever, so you have to enjoy them while they’re here.”

“But I want to fly forever,” whispered Pip.

“Maybe you don’t need to fly forever,” said Silas. “Maybe you just need to fly well.”

Pip hopped up onto a low stone. She felt the breeze on her damp back. She did not jump right away. She waited. She watched a tiny feather in the wind. It lifted gently, then drifted down. She let her own wings open and feel the air.

“Okay,” she said softly. “I’ll try your way.”

She did not shoot up like a rocket. She pushed off gently. The breeze caught her, lifted her a little, and she let it. She flew in a wide, slow circle. Then she saw a big, soft mushroom cap. It looked like a perfect landing pad.

Pip slowed her wings. She tilted her body down. She aimed. *Plop.* Her little feet hit the mushroom with a soft, cushiony sound. She wobbled once… and then she stood still. She had landed.

“I did it!” she chirped.

Silas smiled from his spot on the ground. He carefully unrolled his map and added a tiny star next to the Wiggly River Puddle. “You were brave today, Pip,” he said.

Pip hopped down next to him. “But I didn’t go very high,” she said.

“Going high isn’t the bravest part,” said Silas. “Waiting to learn how to come down safely… that is the kind of bravery that takes a very long time. And you did it.”

Pip looked at the old oak tree. It still looked tall. But now it didn’t look scary. It looked like a promise she would keep one day—after she learned every landing in the garden.

“Show me your map,” she said.

And so the careful snail and the young sparrow sat together in the warm, wet garden. Silas showed her where every puddle would be, and Pip told him how the wind had felt on her feathers. And the sun slowly dried the Shiny Puddle, the Giant Puddle, and the Wiggly River Puddle, one by one, until only the memory of the rain remained—and a new friendship, as patient and brave as the summer afternoon.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Opens New “Pollinator Path” to Teach Public About Native Plant Habitats**

**HARBORLEAF, CA — July 12, 2026** — Harborleaf Botanical Garden today announced the opening of its newest public education initiative, the “Pollinator Path,” a dedicated 0.8-mile accessible trail designed to help visitors understand how small planting choices can support local bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds. The trail, located on the east meadow loop of the garden’s grounds, officially opened to the public this morning.

The Pollinator Path was developed in direct response to a growing number of visitor inquiries seeking practical, hands-on guidance for creating habitat-friendly gardens at home. Garden staff noted that while many guests were enthusiastic about supporting pollinators, they often lacked clear information about which native plants to use, how to arrange them, or how to maintain year-round resources for these species.

“We kept hearing the same question: ‘What can I actually plant in my yard that will help the butterflies and bees?’” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “The Pollinator Path is our answer. It’s a living classroom that shows visitors, step by step, how even small planting decisions—like choosing one native shrub over an exotic ornamental—can create connected habitats across neighborhoods. When we link our gardens, we build corridors for pollinators to travel and thrive.”

The trail meanders through the garden’s east meadow, a site chosen for its existing ecological diversity and open access. Designed with universal accessibility in mind, the path features a smooth, packed-earth surface suitable for strollers, wheelchairs, and mobility aids. Along the route, visitors will encounter a series of interpretive stations and demonstration areas:

- **Native plant demonstration beds** showcase a curated selection of regional wildflowers, grasses, and shrubs that provide nectar, pollen, and larval host material for pollinators throughout the growing season.
- **A night-blooming flower station** highlights species that open after dusk, such as evening primrose and moonflower, which attract moths and other nocturnal pollinators. The station includes subtle low-level lighting to allow evening viewing without disrupting insect activity.
- **Tactile signage** at each station features raised text, braille, and textured plant illustrations, ensuring the trail is informative for visitors with visual impairments.
- **Youth observation cards** are available at the trailhead, encouraging children and families to record sightings of specific pollinators, note flower colors visited, and track seasonal changes. Cards can be returned to the garden’s visitor center for a small reward.
- **Shaded resting points** with benches are spaced every 0.2 miles, offering respite and opportunities for quiet observation.
- **Volunteer-led weekend walks** will be offered on Saturday and Sunday mornings throughout the summer, led by trained garden docents who can answer questions in real time and deepen visitors’ understanding of pollination ecology.

The Pollinator Path is part of Harborleaf Botanical Garden’s broader commitment to environmental education and community engagement. Unlike many commercial or political campaigns, the trail focuses solely on ecological literacy and practical gardening advice. It does not endorse any political candidates, funding sources, or celebrity ambassadors. Instead, it aims to empower individuals with knowledge they can apply in their own backyards, schoolyards, or community spaces.

“We’re not asking anyone to redesign their entire yard overnight,” Ortiz added. “We’re simply showing that one small change—a pot of native milkweed on an apartment balcony, a patch of clover in a sunny corner—can make a real difference. When thousands of people make that small change, the result is a network of tiny habitats that together support regional pollinator populations.”

The trail’s design was informed by input from local ecologists, horticulturists, and accessibility specialists. Harborleaf Botanical Garden staff worked to ensure that the path remains nonpolitical and welcoming to all visitors, regardless of gardening experience or background. The demonstration beds will be maintained by garden volunteers and updated seasonally to reflect the changing availability of blooming plants.

Visitors to the Pollinator Path can access the trail from the east meadow parking area during regular garden hours, 9 a.m. to sunset. The path is free with general garden admission, and weekend walks require no advance registration. Group tours for schools or community organizations may be arranged by contacting the learning programs office.

Harborleaf Botanical Garden, established in 1972, is a nonprofit public garden dedicated to the conservation and display of native plants. Its 45-acre site includes multiple themed gardens, a research library, and year-round educational programming.

For more information about the Pollinator Path or to schedule a group visit, please contact:

**Media Contact:**  
Lena Chu  
Communications Coordinator  
Harborleaf Botanical Garden  
555-010-2300 ext. 104  
lchu@harborleafbotanical.org  
www.harborleafbotanical.org  

# # #

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The laundromat on Harbor Street was a tomb of white machines under a gray sky. A power outage had silenced their usual hum, leaving only the rhythmic complaint of the sea and the thin, keening sound of the wind through a cracked windowpane. Inside, the light came from a single battery lantern on the folding counter, its beam a warm, miniature sun against the encroaching dusk.

Leo had come for the towels. A gesture of practicality in a weekend otherwise defined by silence. The spin cycle had died mid-sentence, leaving a load of damp, twisted terry cloth hostage in machine number four. He was trying to pry the door open with a dull butter knife from the car, muttering a low, patient stream of profanity, when the laundromat’s bell jingled.

He didn’t turn. Not at first. The sound of a woman’s quiet sigh, however, was unmistakable. It was a sound of weary, bone-deep recognition.

“Leo.”

He knew that voice. It was the voice of a summer he’d spent burning sage on a second-floor balcony, the voice that had read him passages from a crumbling paperback over the fence that separated their yards. It was a voice he had spent three years un-learning.

He turned. Iris stood in the doorway, a wicker basket propped on her hip, her dark hair tucked under a woolen beanie. The lantern light gilded the fine lines around her eyes and the crisp line of her jaw. She looked older, not in years but in something hardened around the edges, the way a shore rock is shaped by constant water.

“Iris.” Her name came out rough, as if pulled from a drawer he’d kept locked.

A long silence stretched between them, filled by the distant crash of a wave. She set her basket down with a deliberate thud. “I forgot the area had a blackout tonight. I was coming from the campsite up the road. I have a generator, but I needed… a dryer.” She gestured vaguely at the dark, sleeping machines.

“They’re all down,” he said, turning back to his assault on machine four. “Been out for an hour. Power company says maybe two more.”

He felt her approach before he saw her. The air shifted, carrying the ghost of salt and pine sap. She stopped beside him, close enough that he could see the frayed cuff of her sweater.

“You’re going to break the lock,” she said, her voice flat.

“It’s already broken. It’s broken by design.” He gave up on the knife and shoved his hand in his pocket. A bead of sweat rolled down his temple, though the room was cold.

She reached past him and, with a fluid, familiar motion, pressed a hidden button on the machine’s rim. The door clicked open with a soft hiss.

He stared at the open door, then at her hand. “Some things don’t change.”

“No,” she said. The word was heavy. “Some things just sit there, waiting for the right latch.”

He pulled the damp towels out, one by one, piling them in a messy heap on the table. The lantern made their shadows long and distorted. She didn’t move to help. Instead, she pulled out her phone, her face lit by a cold blue glow.

“I found something tonight,” she said, her voice softer now. “Cleaning out an old drive.” She turned the phone toward him. It was a voicemail icon. The timestamp was three years and four months old. The day he’d left. He hadn’t said goodbye. He’d packed a car at dawn, left a key under the mat, and driven three states away without a note.

“I never listened to it,” she continued. “I didn’t want to. I thought… if it was a goodbye, I didn’t want to hear it. And if it wasn’t…” She trailed off.

“Iris.” The word was a plea.

“Listen to it,” she said. It was not a request.

He took the phone. It was warm. He pressed the play button, holding it to his ear. The line crackled. Then, his own voice, younger, rougher, strained with a panic he’d long since buried.

*“Iris. It’s me. I know it’s early. Listen. I… I can’t stay. It’s not you. It’s the rows of empty houses and the silence in my throat. I have to leave before I become the kind of man who stays out of habit and hates you for it. I’m sorry. I’m so sorry. Don’t hate me. Please. That’s all… that’s all I ask.”*

The message ended. The silence that followed was absolute.

He handed the phone back, his hand trembling slightly. He didn’t know how to look at her. He had imagined this moment a thousand times, but always with words he’d rehearsed, with an apology that sounded clean and final. Now, all he had was a pile of damp, useless towels and a knot in his chest like a fist.

“It wasn’t a goodbye,” she said quietly. Her voice was not warm, but it was no longer flat. It was the voice of someone performing an autopsy on a wound that had never properly healed.

“It was,” he said. “It was a coward’s goodbye.”

She turned off her phone and tucked it away. She picked up one of his damp towels and began to fold it, precisely, corner to corner. “I spent a year thinking I’d done something. Said something. I checked every conversation we ever had, looking for the moment I pushed you away.”

“You didn’t push,” he said, the words coming out ragged. “It was me. I was drowning in the quiet. And I thought the only way to stop drowning was to leave the water.”

“And now?” she asked, her hands still moving over the cloth.

He didn’t have an answer. He looked at the lantern, its steady, unblinking light. “Now, I live in a city two hours inland. I have a job in a hardware store. I come here on weekends because I miss the salt.”

She set the folded towel on the counter. A single, neat square. “I missed your voice. I didn’t realize how much until I heard it just now. I didn’t miss you as a person I knew. I missed you as a shape in my life that I didn’t get to say goodbye to.”

He wanted to reach for her hand. He wanted to say he was sorry until the words lost their shape. But he knew, with the cruel clarity of this small, dark room, that an apology was a demand for forgiveness, and forgiveness was not his to take. She had earned the right to withhold it.

He picked up the folded towel. It was warm and heavy in his hands. “I don’t expect anything,” he said, his voice barely above a whisper. “I just… I’m glad you listened.”

Iris looked at him for a long time, her face unreadable in the amber light. Then she turned, walked to her wicker basket, and lifted it. She paused at the door, her back to him.

“The tide is low tonight,” she said. “You can see the old pier. The one that washed up last winter.”

She didn’t wait for an answer. She opened the door, letting in a gust of cold air. The bell jingled. And then she was gone, swallowed by the dark.

Leo was left alone with the lantern, the damp towels, and the phone in his pocket that had never rung with her voice. He didn’t follow her. He simply stood there, holding the folded towel, listening to the sea, and letting the unresolved weight of the moment settle into his bones like a stone dropped into deep water.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# The Multi-Tenant Balance: Designing Rate Limits That Protect Without Punishing

Backend engineers often conflate several related but distinct concepts when designing API access control. For a multi-tenant API serving both small teams and enterprise customers, getting this wrong means either allowing a noisy integration to degrade service for everyone, or frustrating legitimate users with false positives. This article breaks down how to design rate limiting that distinguishes between authentication, quotas, and limits—and that scales in fairness across tenants of wildly different sizes.

## 1. Authentication, Authorization, Quotas, and Rate Limits: The Four Layers

Most API design discussions blur these terms, but each serves a separate purpose:

**Authentication** answers *who you are*. It’s the token, API key, or OAuth handshake that identifies the client. Without it, no other layer can act.

**Authorization** answers *what you are allowed to do*. It’s the policy layer: can this tenant write to the analytics endpoint? Can this user delete other users’ data? Authorization is about permissions, not volume.

**Quotas** answer *how much you are allowed to consume over a long period*. Quotas are contractual limits: “Your plan allows 1 million requests per month.” They are typically enforced as hard caps after the fact, or as soft warnings. Quotas are rarely enforced in real-time because they are tied to billing.

**Rate limits** answer *how fast you are allowed to consume*. They protect the system’s stability moment-to-moment. A quota might say “10,000 requests per month,” but a rate limit says “no more than 100 requests per second.” Rate limits are the circuit breaker that prevents a single tenant from overwhelming shared infrastructure.

In a multi-tenant system, rate limits must be tenant-aware and plan-aware. A small team with 10 requests/second should not be treated the same as an enterprise tenant with 1,000 requests/second, but both must be protected from abusive behavior.

## 2. Token Bucket vs. Fixed Window vs. Sliding Window: Practical Trade-offs

The three common algorithms behave quite differently under real traffic patterns.

**Fixed window** divides time into discrete buckets (e.g., one per minute). It’s simple to implement and understand. However, it suffers from “burst at the boundary” problems: if a tenant sends 100 requests at 11:59:59 and another 100 at 12:00:01, they effectively get 200 requests in two seconds. This defeats the purpose of rate limiting. Fixed windows are acceptable only for very coarse limits with wide windows, and even then only as a first approximation.

**Sliding window log** (or sliding window counter) tracks timestamps per request and slides the window continuously. It solves the boundary problem but introduces memory overhead—each request creates a record. For high-throughput services, this becomes expensive to store and query. Many implementations approximate it with a counter and a timestamp of the window start, which reintroduces some boundary issues.

**Token bucket** is the gold standard for multi-tenant API rate limiting. It models a bucket that fills at a steady rate (refill rate) and holds a maximum number of tokens (burst capacity). A tenant can use tokens as fast as they want until the bucket empties, then must wait for refill. The key advantage: a tenant with low average usage can still handle sudden bursts (e.g., a webhook flood), while a tenant that saturates the refill rate is smoothly throttled. Token bucket is simple to implement with a counter and a last-refill timestamp per tenant, has bounded memory, and naturally allows bursts—exactly what small teams need and large enterprises require.

**Recommendation**: Implement token bucket per tenant, with bucket size (burst) and refill rate configurable per plan. For enterprise tenants, increase both parameters; for small teams, keep them modest.

## 3. Avoiding Punishment of an Entire Tenant for One Noisy Integration

The worst outcome in multi-tenant rate limiting: a single customer’s misbehaving integration (e.g., an infinite retry loop) exhausts the tenant’s global rate limit, blocking all other legitimate integrations for that tenant. This is not just an engineering failure—it’s a customer support disaster.

The solution is **hierarchical or nested rate limits**. Implement at least two tiers:

- **Per-API-key level**: Each integration (API key) has its own token bucket. A noisy key can exhaust its own bucket and be throttled.
- **Per-tenant level**: A second, larger bucket that sums across all API keys for that tenant. This prevents a single key from consuming the tenant’s entire allocation, but also prevents the tenant from exceeding its total plan limit.

When a per-key bucket empties, the client for that key receives a 429. Other keys for the same tenant continue unaffected. Only when the tenant-level bucket is exhausted do all keys for that tenant get throttled—and only then should an alert fire, because that suggests a systemic problem (e.g., the tenant’s total usage exceeds their contracted plan, or there is a DDoS).

Additionally, implement a **concurrency limiter** alongside the rate limiter. Rate limits control request volume over time; concurrency limits control how many simultaneous connections or inflight requests a single key can maintain. A retry loop that keeps connections open can exhaust server resources faster than a rate limit can catch it. A concurrency limit (e.g., max 5 inflight requests per API key) acts as a softer, faster-acting backpressure.

## 4. Headers and Error Messages: Transparency for Clients

Rate limit feedback must be clear, actionable, and consistent. The industry-standard headers are:

- `X-RateLimit-Limit`: The maximum number of requests allowed in the current window (or the burst capacity for token bucket). For per-key limits, this is the key’s bucket size.
- `X-RateLimit-Remaining`: The number of requests remaining in the current bucket. This lets clients self-throttle.
- `X-RateLimit-Reset`: A Unix timestamp indicating when the bucket will fully refill (or the window will reset). For token bucket, you can calculate this as `now + (empty_tokens / refill_rate)`.
- `Retry-After`: If a 429 is returned, the number of seconds the client should wait before retrying. This is mandatory for proper client backoff.

The 429 response body should include a structured JSON object:

```json
{
  "error": {
    "code": "rate_limit_exceeded",
    "message": "API rate limit exceeded for API key 'abc123'. Retry after 30 seconds.",
    "rate_limit": {
      "limit": 100,
      "remaining": 0,
      "reset_at": 1735689600
    }
  }
}
```

Distinguish between per-key and per-tenant limits in the error message. If a per-key limit is hit, say so explicitly. If the tenant-level limit is hit, include the tenant ID and a message like “Your tenant’s total request volume has exceeded its plan limit.”

## 5. Observability: Metrics, Logs, and Alerts

Rate limiting is only useful if you know it’s working—and not blocking traffic you want.

**Metrics to track** (per tenant, per API key, aggregated):

- **Requests total**: Total requests served (not throttled).
- **Requests throttled**: Count of 429 responses.
- **Throttle rate**: Throttled / Total, monitored per tenant.
- **Token bucket fill level**: Average usage vs. refill rate. This tells you how close each tenant is to their limit under normal conditions.
- **Concurrency**: Current in-flight requests per key.

**Logs** should record every 429 with: tenant ID, API key, endpoint, request path, rate limit tier hit (key vs. tenant), and the token bucket state. This allows post-mortem analysis of whether a noisy integration was legitimately abusive or the limit was too tight.

**Alerts** – set thresholds carefully:

- **Per-key throttle rate > 30%** for a given key over 5 minutes: likely a misbehaving client. Notify the tenant’s team or auto-disable the key after confirmation.
- **Per-tenant throttle rate > 50%**: critical. This means the tenant is hitting its global limit, which blocks all their integrations. Investigate whether the tenant needs a plan upgrade or has a compromised key.
- **System-wide throttle rate > 10%**: the infrastructure may be under-provisioned, or a burst of legitimate traffic is being blocked incorrectly.

Crucially, track false positives. Compare throttled requests against client retry behavior: if clients retry after 429 and succeed, the limit is working. If they retry and still get 429, the throttle may be too aggressive for their use case. Monitor customer support tickets mentioning “429” to correlate.

The goal of observability is not just to see when limits are hit, but to verify that the limits are set at the right level: protecting reliability while allowing legitimate traffic to pass smoothly. If a small team’s integration consistently operates at 95% of its limit, consider raising their limit or suggesting a plan upgrade—not forcing them to refactor.

## Recommended Baseline Design

For a multi-tenant API serving small teams and enterprise customers, start with this configuration:

1. **Per API key**: Token bucket with burst = 50, refill rate = 10/second (allows short bursts but sustainable at 10 rps).
2. **Per tenant**: Token bucket with burst = 500, refill rate = 100/second (sum of all keys).
3. **Concurrency limiter**: Max 10 in-flight requests per API key.
4. **Enterprise plan overrides**: Custom bucket sizes (e.g., burst = 1000, refill = 200/s) configured per tenant.
5. **Headers**: `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`, `Retry-After` on 429.
6. **Observability**: Metrics for per-key and per-tenant throttle rates; alert when per-tenant throttle exceeds 50% over 5 minutes.

This design gives small teams the burst capacity they need for normal operations while preventing any single integration from degrading service for others. Enterprise tenants get room to scale, but still have hard boundaries that protect the shared infrastructure. The key insight: rate limiting should be as fine-grained as your authentication model allows—per key, per user, per endpoint—and always with a tenant-level safety net.

请帮我写一个单文件 HTML 水族箱动画。

要求：
1. 画面里有多条不同颜色、不同大小的鱼，游动要尽量自然。
2. 用户左键点击水族箱时，可以在点击位置放下一粒鱼食。
3. 每条鱼会追逐离自己最近的鱼食并尝试吃掉它；没有鱼食时恢复自由游动。
4. 需要有完整的 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript，能直接保存为一个 `.html` 文件运行。
5. 视觉效果要精致一些，不要只给伪代码或解释，直接给出完整代码。

请帮我创建一个单文件 HTML 时钟应用，要求直接给出完整代码。

功能和视觉要求：
1. 时钟表盘是圆形，有刻度、数字、时针、分针、秒针，并在表盘内部显示当前时间和日期。
2. 表盘需要有简洁的高级感：白色背景、深色边框、轻微 3D 阴影。
3. 用 JavaScript 动态生成 60 个刻度，整点刻度更粗更深。
4. 时间必须转换为中国标准时间（北京时区，Asia/Shanghai）。
5. 写一个 updateClock() 函数，计算时针、分针、秒针角度；秒针需要尽量平滑运动。
6. 使用 setInterval 或 requestAnimationFrame 驱动刷新。
7. 代码需要兼容 Edge 浏览器。

请将下面的中文商务写作材料翻译成英文。要求：面向现代职场读者，语气清楚、专业、克制；不要翻译成生硬腔调；保留作者关于“商务信函应传达信息并建立善意”的核心观点。

素材来源：根据公共领域英文书《The Book of Business Etiquette》相关章节改写的中文素材
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists the source eBook as public domain in the USA; this Chinese passage is a benchmark rewrite.

【待处理素材】
许多商务信函本不必写，而已经写出的信中，又有不少因为信息残缺、结构混乱，使原本两封信便能完成并归档的交易，拖成六封或八封往返。好的信件来自清楚的思考和周密的安排。销售函有时需要数周打磨；普通业务往来只需几分钟也足够。关键不在于花了多久，而在于最终写出的信是否能准确完成任务。

衡量商务信函的规则很多，但真正不可缺少的只有两点：第一，信必须让收件人明白你的意思；第二，它必须为公司建立善意。最好的信，是用最简短、最礼貌、最完整的方式做到这两点。每封信背后都应有明确理由，哪怕只是向客户说一声谢谢。许多精力浪费在无目的的广告、无效的推销和多余的来信上；这些文字占用了打字机和纸张，却没有推动任何事情前进。

一封好信不应让读者注意它本身的文采，而应让读者接收到它要传达的消息。某出版社曾寄出一批推销新书的测试信，回信者称赞信写得漂亮，却无人订购图书。这说明文字若抢走了信息的焦点，再优雅也不是成功的商务写作。信纸、信头、版式、段落都应整洁、克制、清楚；过分花哨的设计和故作新奇的格式，只会让人分心。

商务写作还应尊重读者的时间。清楚的标题、适当的称呼、按主题分段的正文、明确的请求和完整的联系方式，往往比华丽措辞更能建立信任。若一封信需要收件人回复，写信者应说明希望对方在何时以前回复、需要提供哪些信息，以及下一步将如何处理。这样，信件不仅传递态度，也推动事务向前。

message.content:
Many business letters should never have been written in the first place. And among those that are written, a significant number suffer from missing information or disorganized structure—turning what could have been a two-letter transaction into six or eight exchanges. Good letters come from clear thinking and careful planning. While a sales letter may require weeks of refinement, a routine business correspondence can be effectively written in minutes. What matters is not how long it takes, but whether the final letter does its job.

There are many rules for evaluating business correspondence, but only two are truly essential. First, the letter must make your meaning clear to the reader. Second, it must build goodwill for your company. The best letters accomplish both goals in the shortest, most courteous, and most complete manner. Every letter should have a clear reason behind it, even if it is simply to say thank you to a customer. Too much effort is wasted on aimless advertisements, ineffective sales pitches, and unnecessary correspondence—tying up typewriters and paper without moving anything forward.

A good letter should not draw attention to its own style; it should direct the reader’s attention to the message it conveys. A publisher once sent out a batch of test letters promoting a new book. Recipients praised the writing as elegant—yet no one placed an order. This illustrates that when the words steal focus from the content, no amount of polish can make the writing successful. Letterhead, layout, formatting, and paragraph structure should all be clean, restrained, and clear. Overly flashy design or gimmicky formatting only distracts.

Business writing should also respect the reader’s time. Clear subject lines, appropriate salutations, well-organized paragraphs by topic, explicit requests, and complete contact details build trust more effectively than ornate language. If the letter requires a response, the writer should indicate by when a reply is needed, what information is required, and what the next steps will be. In this way, the letter does more than convey an attitude—it moves the matter forward.

messages.content:
请将下面的繁体中文寓言/哲理片段翻译成英文，目标读者为青少年读者。要求：保留想象力和节奏；遇到典故不要长篇解释；译文应清楚、流畅，适合作为儿童文学或寓言文本阅读。

素材来源：《莊子的故事》节选：逍遥游开篇
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23913
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
北冥有魚，其名為鯤。鯤之大，不知其幾千里也。化而為鳥，其名為鵬。鵬之背，不知其幾千里也；怒而飛，其翼若垂天之雲。是鳥也，海運則將徙於南冥。南冥者，天池也。齊諧者，志怪者也。諧之言曰：「鵬之徙於南冥也，水擊三千里，摶扶搖而上者九萬里，去以六月息者也。」野馬也，塵埃也，生物之以息相吹也。天之蒼蒼，其正色邪？其遠而無所至極邪？其視下也，亦若是則已矣。且夫水之積也不厚，則其負大舟也無力。覆杯水於坳堂之上，則芥為之舟；置杯焉則膠，水淺而舟大也。風之積也不厚，則其負大翼也無力。故九萬里，則風斯在下矣，而後乃今掊風；背負青天而莫之夭閼者，而後乃今將圖南。蜩與學鳩笑之曰：「我決起而飛，槍榆枋而止，時則不至而控於地而已矣，奚以這九萬里而南為？」適莽蒼者，三餐而反，腹猶果然；適百里者，宿舂糧；適千里者，三月聚糧。之二蟲又何知！小知不及大知，小年不及大年。奚以知其然也？朝菌不知晦朔，蟪蛄不知春秋，此小年也。楚之南有冥靈者，以五百歲為春，五百歲為秋；上古有大椿者，以八千歲為春，八千歲為秋。此大年也。而彭祖乃今以久特聞，眾人匹之，不亦悲乎！湯之問棘也是已。窮髮之北有冥海者，天池也。有魚焉，其廣數千里，未有知其脩者，其名為鯤。有鳥焉，其名為鵬，背若泰山，翼若垂天之雲，摶扶搖羊角而上者九萬里，絕雲氣，負青天，然後圖南，且適南冥也。斥鴳笑之曰：「彼且奚適也？我騰躍而上，不過數仞而下，翱翔蓬蒿之間，此亦飛之至也，而彼且奚適也？」此小大之辯也。故夫知效一官，行比一鄉，德合一君，而徵一國者，其自視也亦若此矣。而宋榮子猶然笑之。且舉世而譽之而不加勸，舉世而非之而不加沮，定乎內外之分，辯乎榮辱之竟，斯已矣。彼其於世，未數數然也。雖然，猶有未樹也。夫列子御風而行，泠然善也，旬有五日而反。彼於致福者，未數數然也。此雖免乎行，猶有所待者也。若夫乘天地之正，而御六氣之辯，以遊無窮者，彼且惡乎待哉！故曰：至人無己，神人無功，聖人無名。

message.content:
In the Northern Ocean there is a fish, called Kun. How big is Kun? No one knows—thousands of miles long, perhaps. It transforms into a bird, named Peng. And Peng’s back—how vast it is! Thousands of miles across. When it rises in fury and takes flight, its wings spread like clouds hanging from the sky. This bird rides the ocean currents and migrates to the Southern Ocean—the Celestial Pool.

There is a book called *Qi Xie*, a collection of strange tales. It says: "When Peng travels to the Southern Ocean, it churns the water for three thousand miles, and soars on a whirlwind ninety thousand miles high. It leaves in the sixth month, riding the great wind."

Like wild horses galloping, like dust motes dancing—all living things are blown about by the breath of the universe. The sky’s deep blue—is that its true color? Or is it simply so far away that we cannot reach its end? Peng, looking down from above, sees only the same endless blue.

If water is not deep enough, it cannot carry a great ship. Pour a cup of water into a hollow in the floor—a mustard seed becomes a boat. But if you place a cup there, it sticks fast, because the water is shallow and the vessel is heavy. So too, if the wind is not thick enough, it cannot support great wings. Peng needed the wind ninety thousand miles deep beneath it. Only then could it ride the wind, with the blue sky on its back, nothing blocking its way—and finally head south.

The cicada and the little dove laughed at Peng. "We take off in a flash, flutter among the elm and willow trees, and if we don’t make it, we just drop to the ground. Why go ninety thousand miles south?"

If you are just going to the countryside, you take three meals and come back still full. If you go a hundred miles, you grind grain the night before. If you go a thousand miles, you store up food for three months. Those two little creatures—what do they know?

Small knowledge cannot match great knowledge; short lives cannot match long ones. How do we know? The morning mushroom knows nothing of dawn and dusk; the cicada knows nothing of spring and autumn. That is short life. In the south of Chu, there is a tree called Mingling—five hundred years is its spring, five hundred years its autumn. In ancient times, there was a great Chun tree—eight thousand years for its spring, eight thousand for its autumn. That is long life. And yet Peng Zu is famous for his long life—how sad that people compare themselves to him!

Tang, the legendary emperor, asked Ji about this. In the barren north, there is a dark sea—the Celestial Pool. A fish lives there, thousands of miles wide—no one knows how long. Its name is Kun. And a bird named Peng has a back like Mount Tai, wings like clouds hanging from the sky. It rides a whirlwind ninety thousand miles high, piercing the clouds, carrying the blue sky on its back, then heads south—to the Southern Ocean.

The little quail laughed at it. "Where does it think it’s going? I leap and fly, no more than a few yards up, then flutter among the weeds. That’s the best flying there is! And where does it think it’s going?"

This is the difference between small and great.

And so, those whose wisdom fits a single office, whose conduct suits a single village, whose virtue pleases a single ruler, who win trust in a single state—they see themselves exactly like that little quail. Song Rongzi laughed at them. Even if the whole world praised him, he wouldn’t be encouraged; if the whole world criticized him, he wouldn’t be discouraged. He knew the difference between inner and outer, and understood where true honor and shame lie. That was all. He never chased worldly fame. Yet even he had not planted his feet firmly.

Liezi could ride the wind—light and graceful—and return after fifteen days. He was not concerned with seeking fortune. Yet he still depended on something—the wind itself.

But one who rides the true nature of heaven and earth, who harnesses the changes of the six elements, and wanders through the infinite—what does such a one need to depend on?

That is why it is said: The Perfect Person has no self. The Holy Person has no merit. The Sage has no fame.

messages.content:
请将下面的中文新闻稿素材翻译成英文。目标读者为普通英语新闻读者。要求：准确、自然、新闻稿风格；不要添加事实；保留机构名的含义，可译为 U.S. Geological Survey；不要使用夸张宣传语。

素材来源：改写自 U.S. Geological Survey 水循环图发布新闻稿
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/news/national-news-release/new-usgs-diagram-re-envisions-how-earths-most-precious-commodity-cycles
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain; this Chinese source is a benchmark rewrite based on that public-domain material.

【待处理素材】
美国地质调查局发布新版水循环教学图，帮助教育者更完整地解释地球水在自然系统与人类活动之间的流动。新版图示取代了自2000年以来被大量课堂使用的旧版本。旧图多以一个大循环表现蒸发、降水、径流等自然过程，而新版把人类用水、土地利用、蓄水工程、灌溉和地下水开采等因素纳入同一张图中，展示许多小循环如何彼此连接、相互影响。

该图的开发团队咨询了一百多名教育工作者和三十多名水文学专家，并参考了美国地质调查局长期积累的水文数据。新版图示不仅说明水如何在大气、地表和地下储存、转移和改变形态，也强调水量、水质和水到达时间对可用水资源的影响。例如，降水并不会简单地从天空落下再回到海洋；一部分水会进入土壤和含水层，一部分会被植物吸收并通过蒸腾回到空气中，一部分会被水库、农田、城市排水系统和家庭用水改变路径。

设计者采用信息设计原则，让读者能顺着水的路径观察沿海平原、干旱盆地、湿润盆地和农业盆地之间的联系。图中还强调，不同区域的用水选择会影响下游水量和水质，因此理解水循环不能只看自然过程，也要观察人类活动如何改变水的储存位置、移动速度和清洁程度。新版图示最初提供英文和西班牙文版本，预期将由使用者翻译成更多语言。发布方表示，这一工具旨在帮助学生、教师和自然资源管理者以更清晰的方式理解水的复杂旅程，并认识到日常用水选择会影响水资源的可持续利用。

message.content:
**U.S. Geological Survey Releases Updated Water Cycle Diagram for Educators**

The U.S. Geological Survey has released a new educational diagram of the water cycle, designed to help educators provide a more comprehensive explanation of how Earth's water moves through both natural systems and human activities. The updated diagram replaces a version that had been widely used in classrooms since 2000.

The previous diagram primarily depicted a single, large cycle showing natural processes such as evaporation, precipitation, and runoff. The new version incorporates human water use, land use, storage infrastructure, irrigation, and groundwater extraction into a single visual. It illustrates how many smaller cycles are connected and interact with one another.

The diagram was developed with input from more than 100 educators and over 30 hydrology experts, and it draws on long-term hydrological data collected by the U.S. Geological Survey. In addition to illustrating how water is stored, transferred, and transformed across the atmosphere, land surface, and underground, the new diagram emphasizes how water quantity, quality, and timing affect the availability of water resources.

For example, precipitation does not simply fall from the sky and return to the ocean. Some water enters soil and aquifers, some is taken up by plants and released back into the air through transpiration, and some is diverted by reservoirs, farmlands, urban drainage systems, and household use.

Designers applied principles of information design to allow viewers to follow water pathways across interconnected landscapes, including coastal plains, arid basins, humid basins, and agricultural basins. The diagram also highlights how water-use decisions in one region can affect downstream water quantity and quality. Understanding the water cycle, the diagram suggests, requires observing not only natural processes but also how human activities alter where water is stored, how quickly it moves, and how clean it is.

The new diagram is initially available in English and Spanish, and it is expected that users will translate it into additional languages. The U.S. Geological Survey stated that the tool is intended to help students, teachers, and natural resource managers better understand the complex journey of water and recognize that everyday water-use choices affect the sustainable availability of water resources.

messages.content:
请将下面的繁体中文小说片段翻译成英文。要求：保留人物心理、礼仪分寸和环境细节；英文要自然、有文学感，但不要过度现代化；人名可采用拼音，例如 Lin Daiyu、Jia residence。不要添加解释性段落。

素材来源：《紅樓夢》节选：林黛玉进贾府
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/24264
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
且說黛玉自那日棄舟登岸時，便有榮國府打發了轎子并拉行李的車輛久候了．這林黛玉常听得母親說過，他外祖母家与別家不同．他近日所見的這几個三等仆婦，吃穿用度，已是不凡了，何況今至其家．因此步步留心，時時在意，不肯輕易多說一句話，多行一步路，惟恐被人恥笑了他去．自上了轎，進入城中從紗窗向外瞧了一瞧，其街市之繁華，人煙之阜盛，自与別處不同．又行了半日，忽見街北蹲著兩個大石獅子，三間獸頭大門，門前列坐著十來個華冠麗服之人．正門卻不開，只有東西兩角門有人出入．正門之上有一匾，匾上大書"敕造宁國府"五個大字．黛玉想道：這必是外祖之長房了．想著，又往西行，不多遠，照樣也是三間大門，方是榮國府了．卻不進正門，只進了西邊角門．那轎夫抬進去，走了一射之地，將轉彎時，便歇下退出去了．后面的婆子們已都下了轎，赶上前來．另換了三四個衣帽周全十七八歲的小廝上來，复抬起轎子．眾婆子步下圍隨至一垂花門前落下．眾小廝退出，眾婆子上來打起轎帘，扶黛玉下轎．林黛玉扶著婆子的手，進了垂花門，兩邊是抄手游廊，當中是穿堂，當地放著一個紫檀架子大理石的大插屏．轉過插屏，小小的三間廳，廳后就是后面的正房大院．正面五間上房，皆雕梁畫棟，兩邊穿山游廊廂房，挂著各色鸚鵡，畫眉等鳥雀．台磯之上，坐著几個穿紅著綠的丫頭，一見他們來了，便忙都笑迎上來，說：“剛才老太太還念呢，可巧就來了。”于是三四人爭著打起帘籠，一面听得人回話：“林姑娘到了。”黛玉方進入房時，只見兩個人攙著一位鬢發如銀的老母迎上來，黛玉便知是他外祖母．方欲拜見時，早被他外祖母一把摟入怀中，心肝儿肉叫著大哭起來．當下地下侍立之人，無不掩面涕泣，黛玉也哭個不住．一時眾人慢慢解勸住了，黛玉方拜見了外祖母．____此即冷子興所云之史氏太君，賈赦賈政之母也．當下賈母一一指与黛玉：“這是你大舅母，這是你二舅母，這是你先珠大哥的媳婦珠大嫂子。”黛玉一一拜見過．賈母又說：“請姑娘們來．今日遠客才來，可以不必上學去了。”眾人答應了一聲，便去了兩個．不一時，只見三個奶嬤嬤并五六個丫鬟，簇擁著三個姊妹來了．第一個肌膚微丰，合中身材，腮凝新荔，鼻膩鵝脂，溫柔沉默，觀之可親．第二個削肩細腰，長挑身材，鴨蛋臉面，俊眼修眉，顧盼神飛，文彩精華，見之忘俗．第三個身量未足，形容尚小．其釵環裙襖，三人皆是一樣的妝飾．黛玉忙起身迎上來見禮，互相廝認過，大家歸了坐．丫鬟們斟上茶來．不過說些黛玉之母如何得病，如何請醫服藥，如何送死發喪．不免賈母又傷感起來，因說：“我這些儿女，所疼者獨有你母，今日一旦先舍我而去，連面也不能一見，今見了你，我怎不傷心！

message.content: Let us speak of Daiyu. From the day she left the boat and set foot on shore, a sedan-chair from the Rongguo Mansion, along with carts for her luggage, had been waiting for her, and had been so for some time. This Lin Daiyu had often heard her mother say that her grandmother’s household was different from others. The few third-class serving women she had seen of late were already extraordinary in their clothing, food, and manner of living—how much more so, then, the household itself? Thus she was cautious at every step, attentive at every moment, unwilling to speak one word too many or take one step too far, for fear of being ridiculed. Once she had mounted the sedan-chair and entered the city, she looked out through the gauze window. The bustling streets, the dense throngs of people—indeed, it was unlike any other place. After travelling for some time, she suddenly saw, on the north side of the street, two great stone lions crouching, and a grand gate with three openings, topped with beast-shaped ornaments. Before the gate sat a dozen or so men in splendid caps and fine garments. The main gate was not open; only the eastern and western side gates showed signs of coming and going. Above the main gate hung a tablet upon which were inscribed, in large characters, the words: “Imperial Construction, Ningguo Mansion.” Daiyu thought to herself: This must be the elder branch of my grandmother’s family. As she pondered, they continued westward, and after a short distance, there appeared another set of three large gates—this was the Rongguo Mansion. They did not enter through the main gate, however, but passed through the western side gate. The bearers carried the sedan-chair inside for about the distance of an arrow’s flight. When they were about to turn a corner, they set the chair down and withdrew. The old women who had followed behind had already descended from their own chairs and hurried forward. In their place, three or four youths of seventeen or eighteen, neat in cap and clothing, took up the sedan-chair once more. The old women, walking at its side, followed it all the way to a gate with hanging flowers, where it was set down. The youths withdrew, and the old women came forward, lifted the sedan-chair’s curtain, and helped Daiyu alight. Leaning on the hand of an old woman, Lin Daiyu entered through the hanging-flower gate. On either side ran covered corridors; in the center was a passage-hall, and in the middle of the hall stood a large screen of marble set in a frame of red sandalwood. Passing around the screen, they entered a small three-room hall, beyond which lay the main courtyard of the rear compound. Facing them were five main rooms, their beams carved and rafters painted; on either side ran open corridors and side-rooms, hung with cages of parrots, thrushes, and other birds. On the steps sat several maids in red and green. As soon as they saw Daiyu approach, they hurried forward with smiles and said: “Just now the old lady was talking of you—and here you are, as if by chance!” At once, three or four of them vied to lift the door-curtain, while inside someone announced: “Miss Lin has arrived.” The moment Daiyu entered the room, she saw two attendants supporting an old lady with hair as white as silver, coming forward to greet her. Daiyu knew at once that this was her grandmother. She was about to kneel and pay her respects when the old lady gathered her into her arms, crying out “my heart’s flesh! my precious!” and wept loudly. All those standing in attendance could not help but cover their faces and weep as well. Daiyu, too, could not stop crying. After some time, those present gradually coaxed them into calm, and Daiyu finally paid her respects to her grandmother. This was the matriarch, née Shi, of whom Leng Zixing had spoken—the mother of Jia She and Jia Zheng. At this moment, the Lady Dowager pointed to them in turn and said to Daiyu: “This is your elder uncle’s wife; this is your second uncle’s wife; this is the wife of your late elder brother, Zhu.” Daiyu greeted each one in turn. The Lady Dowager then said: “Summon the young ladies. Today our guest has come from afar, so they need not go to school.” The servants acknowledged the order, and two of them went off. Before long, three nurses and five or six maids were seen, escorting three young ladies. The first had a slightly full figure and a moderate height; her cheeks were like fresh lychees, her nose as smooth as goose fat; she was gentle, subdued, and pleasant to behold. The second had sloping shoulders and a slender waist, a tall and graceful figure, an oval face, fine eyes and long brows; her glance was lively and spirited, her bearing refined and elegant—one forgot all vulgarity at the sight of her. The third was not yet fully grown, and her form was still small. As for their hairpins, earrings, skirts, and jackets, the three were all dressed alike. Daiyu quickly rose and went forward to greet them. After they had acknowledged one another, they all took their seats. Maids served tea. Their talk turned to how Daiyu’s mother had fallen ill, how doctors had been summoned and medicine administered, and how the funeral had been conducted. The Lady Dowager could not help grieving once more and said: “Of all my children, the one I cherished most was your mother. And now, before my very eyes, she has left me first, without my even being able to see her face one last time. Now that I see you, how could I not be heartbroken?”
