In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around. Nothing but experience in copying her own notes will teach a stenographer to estimate them correctly so that she will not have to rewrite badly placed letters. It is a little point, but an important one.

Each subject considered in a letter should be treated in a separate paragraph, and each paragraph should be set off from the others by a wider space than that between the lines, double space between the paragraphs when there is single space between the lines, triple space between the paragraphs when there is a double space between the lines, and so on.

A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered. This enables the house receiving the letter to file it so that it can be found when it is needed.

When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin "Dear Mr. Brown" or "My dear Mr. Brown" than "Dear Sir" or "My dear Sir." "Gentlemen" or "Ladies" is sometime used in salutation when a letter is addressed to a group. "Dear Friend" is permissible in general letters sent out to persons of both sexes. Honorary titles should be used in the address when they take the place of "Mr.," such titles as Reverend, Doctor, Honorable (abbreviated to Rev., Dr., Hon.,) and the like. Titles should not be dropped except in the case of personal letters.

Special care should be taken with the outside address. State abbreviations should be used sparingly when there is a chance of confusion as in the case of Ga., Va., La., and Pa. "City" is not sufficient and should never be used. Nor should the name of the state ever be omitted even when the letter is addressed to some other point in the same state, as from New York to Brooklyn. And postage should be complete. A letter on which there is two cents due has placed itself under a pretty severe handicap before it is opened.

It is astonishing how many letters go out every day unsigned, lacking enclosures, carrying the wrong addresses, bearing insufficient postage, and showing other evidences of carelessness and thoughtlessness. In a town in New England last year one of the specialty shops received at Christmas time twenty different lots of money--money orders, stamps, and cash--by mail, not one of which bore the slightest clue to the identity of the sender. Countless times during the year this happens in every mail order house.

message.content:
Here is the structured summary and analysis of the provided passage, tailored for modern business readers.

### Executive Summary (100 words)

Half of all business letters waste time and resources due to poor planning. Effective correspondence results from clear thinking, not length. The two essential goals are conveying the message clearly and building goodwill. Avoid flashy formatting; plain, high-quality paper and standard layouts signal professionalism. A letter must not draw attention to itself at the expense of its message. Handle one subject per letter to aid filing and clarity. Use personalized salutations like “Dear Mr. Brown.” Ensure complete postage and clear addresses. Finally, rigorous proofreading prevents costly errors like missing enclosures or unsigned documents. Efficiency and courtesy are paramount.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing

1.  **Plan Before You Write:** Clear letters result from clear thinking. Rushing leads to multiple, fragmented exchanges (2-3 letters vs. 6-8).
2.  **Prioritize Message Over Style:** The content and its clarity matter more than the writer's cleverness. A beautiful letter that fails to sell the product is useless.
3.  **Embrace Standard Conventions:** Professionalism comes from adhering to accepted norms (format, paper, envelope size), not from flashy or “freakish” variations.
4.  **Write with a Clear Purpose:** Every letter must have a reason (even a thank you). Aimless correspondence is a waste of energy and resources.
5.  **Respect the Reader’s Time:** The best letter accomplishes its goal most courteously and completely in the briefest space. Simplicity, directness, and accuracy show respect.

### Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-era advice | Modern equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| "A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning." | A good email, message, or document is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. |
| "Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste." | A clean, simple, and professional email signature, document template, or slide deck design is always in good taste. |
| "A business letter should handle only one subject." | A single email or Slack message should cover only one topic to ensure clarity and easy searchability. |
| "The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm." | Business communication must be clear (get the idea across) and maintain or enhance the brand’s reputation (build goodwill). |
| "Practically all business letters are typewritten." | Practically all professional business communication is digital (email, chat, documents). |

### Dated Advice and Modern Update

**Dated Advice:** The passage emphasizes physical stationery (paper, window envelopes, postage). It also assumes a hierarchical office where men dictate letters to "irresponsible" young female typists who must be supervised.

**Modern Update (Preserving the Principle):** The underlying principle is **professional presentation and error-free execution**. The modern equivalent is digital formatting and proofreading.
- **Update 1 (Physical to Digital):** Use professional fonts (e.g., Calibri, Arial), proper subject lines in emails, and clean document templates. Ensure PDFs are formatted for all screens. Never use Comic Sans for a client proposal.
- **Update 2 (Typist Supervision):** Modern professionals must personally proofread their own emails before hitting send. Use tools like spell-check, grammar check, and “undo send” features. Master your own software (Outlook, Slack, Teams) so you don't rely on others to fix your presentation.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
Mary Lennox, a disagreeable and sickly child born in India, is sent to live with her uncle at Misselthwaite Manor. Her parents neglected her; her mother, a socialite, handed her to an Ayah who spoiled her. By age six, Mary became tyrannical and selfish. One hot morning, she awakens cross to find a strange servant instead of her Ayah. She throws a tantrum, but no one explains the mystery. Later, she overhears her mother speaking fearfully with a young officer about a deadly cholera outbreak. The Ayah has died, and chaos erupts as servants flee or fall ill. During the panic, Mary hides in the nursery, forgotten. She eats leftover food, drinks strong wine, becomes drowsy, and falls asleep alone.

### Mary’s Character Traits with Supporting Details
- **Disagreeable appearance**: She has a “little thin face,” “thin light hair,” and a “sour expression.”
- **Selfish and tyrannical**: She is described as “as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived” by age six.
- **Bad-tempered**: Upon seeing a strange servant, she “threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her.”
- **Rude and insulting**: While playing alone, she mutters “Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” as the worst insult to a native.
- **Neglected and isolated**: During the cholera panic, she “was forgotten by everyone” and “nobody wanted her.”

### Setting and Emotional Distance/Isolation
The setting—a hot, mysterious morning in colonial India—creates emotional isolation through neglect and fear. Mary’s mother keeps her out of sight, handing her to servants who obey her only to avoid angering the Mem Sahib. During the cholera outbreak, Mary is physically alone: her Ayah dies, servants flee, and her mother panics with others, forgetting her entirely. She hides in the nursery, “forgotten by everyone,” hearing frightening sounds but receiving no comfort. The empty dining room with hastily abandoned food underscores her abandonment. This environment traps her in a lonely, uncared-for state, amplifying her sourness and separation from genuine connection.

### Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary becomes so angry and selfish? Can you find examples in the story that help explain her behavior?
2. How do the servants treat Mary differently from how parents might treat a child? What does this tell you about her family life?
3. Imagine you were in Mary’s place during the cholera outbreak. How would you feel being forgotten by everyone, and what might you do to cope?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Structured English Summary

#### 1. Concise 100-Word Overview
The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water on Earth, which covers 75% of the planet's surface. Water is stored in the atmosphere, on land, and underground, and exists as liquid, solid, or gas. The sun and gravity drive its movement among major reservoirs, or "pools." Oceans hold 96% of Earth's water (saline), while freshwater is stored in lakes, rivers, ice sheets, and groundwater. Water changes form and moves through evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. Human water use, land use, and climate change affect this cycle, influencing water availability and movement.

#### 2. Concept Map (Text-Form)
**Atmosphere** ← (evaporation, evapotranspiration) ← **Oceans** & **Land Surface**  
**Atmosphere** → (precipitation) → **Land Surface** & **Oceans**  
**Land Surface** → (snowmelt, runoff, streamflow) → **Oceans**  
**Land Surface** → (infiltration, groundwater recharge) → **Groundwater**  
**Groundwater** → (natural discharge) → **Rivers, Oceans, Springs**

#### 3. Key Terms (≥8)
- **Water cycle**: The continuous movement of water on Earth among storage pools.  
- **Evaporation**: The process where the sun’s energy turns liquid water into water vapor.  
- **Evapotranspiration**: The main way water moves from land and oceans into the atmosphere.  
- **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail falling from clouds due to gravity.  
- **Runoff**: Water moving across the land surface toward streams and oceans.  
- **Infiltration**: Water moving into the ground from the surface.  
- **Groundwater recharge**: The process by which infiltrated water enters underground aquifers.  
- **Aquifer**: An underground layer where groundwater flows and is stored.  
- **Freshwater**: Non-salty water stored in lakes, rivers, ice sheets, glaciers, and snowpack.  
- **Saline water**: Salty water, mostly found in oceans and saline lakes.

#### 4. Human Influence
Human actions affect water movement, storage, and availability through **water use**, **land use**, and **climate change**. For example, extracting groundwater, building reservoirs, altering landscapes, and changing land cover modify natural flows and storage. These impacts alter how much water is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the cycle, and water quality. Understanding these influences helps work toward sustainable water use.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Operational Update: Warehouse System Migration & Current Service Impact  

**Team,**  

We are in the middle of a planned transition to a new warehouse management system, and I want to share a direct, unvarnished update on how this is affecting our operations and your daily work.  

Over the past seven days, our shipping speed has dropped significantly. Orders that typically leave the warehouse within 24 hours are now taking 3–5 days on average to dispatch. Customer support volume has risen sharply—primarily from “Where is my order?” inquiries and delivery date concerns. I want to give you the facts on why this is happening, what we are doing right now, and what you can expect next week.  

**What Happened**  

This migration was scoped over nine months with cross-functional testing. In controlled environments, system performance was reliable. However, once we went live in the production warehouse environment, we encountered two unanticipated issues:  

1. **Inventory location logic:** The new system assigns pick locations differently than our old system. This caused pickers to route through the warehouse in longer, less efficient paths during the first two days after go-live.  
2. **Label generation latency:** A batch-labeling process that worked in staging now takes 2–3 seconds longer per order during peak volume. For a facility processing thousands of orders daily, those seconds compound into hours of delay.  

Neither issue is a result of a team underperforming or a vendor failing. They are gaps in how our stress-testing simulated real-world, high-volume conditions. The warehouse operations team, engineering, and our vendor’s deployment team are working side-by-side to resolve these with no finger-pointing and no excuses.  

**Current Customer Impact**  

- **Standard orders:** Current average shipping time is 4.2 days (vs. our target of 1 day).  
- **Priority/expedited orders:** These are shipping on day 5–6 (vs. promised 2–3 days).  
- **Customer support contacts:** Up 210% since go-live. Hold times for inbound calls average 11 minutes. Live chat response time is 22 minutes.  
- **Cancellation rate:** Within normal range so far, but return requests are elevated—mostly from customers who ordered for an event that has now passed.  

**What We Are Doing Today**  

- **Warehouse floor adjustments:** We have temporarily reverted to manual pick-path assignment for the highest-volume SKUs, bypassing the new system’s routing logic. This reduces efficiency but restores throughput to 85% of pre-migration levels.  
- **Label process fix:** Engineering identified a configuration change in the print-server queue. We are deploying a hotfix this afternoon. We expect label generation to return to normal by end of shift.  
- **Customer support:** Our VP of CX has approved overtime for the support team through this weekend. Agents are empowered to refund shipping charges automatically and adjust delivery windows without escalation.  
- **Escalation path for urgent issues:** If you are a frontline team member and encounter any scenario that could cause a shipment to be delayed beyond 7 days or a safety risk (e.g., damaged equipment, mislabeled hazmat), escalate immediately to your department lead AND email [urgent-ops@company.com](mailto:urgent-ops@company.com) with “URGENT WMS” in the subject line. We will triage within 30 minutes during operating hours.  

**What Will Change Next Week**  

- **By Monday:** The label-generation hotfix should be fully validated and in production. We expect shipping speed to return to a 2-day average (not yet back to 1 day, but a measurable improvement).  
- **By Wednesday:** The warehouse routing logic will be reconfigured to use a hybrid method—old path data for familiar SKUs, new algorithm for less-common items. This reduces the inefficiency we saw on launch.  
- **By Friday:** We will begin proactively emailing every customer whose order shipped more than 5 days late with a $10 credit code and a revised delivery estimate. This is a one-time program—we are not making this a permanent policy.  

**What I Will Not Promise**  

I will not promise you that shipping will be back to 24 hours by next Friday. That may take two more weeks. I will not promise that there will be no further delays. These fixes are based on current root causes, and new issues may surface. What I can promise is that I will share the exact same data with you next Wednesday in a brief written update, with no spin, no clichés, and no empty assurances.  

**Manager Talking Points (for team huddles this week)**  

To help you address your teams’ questions calmly and consistently, here are four concise points you can share in your next stand-up or shift brief:  

1. **We moved warehouses from one system to another. The system works in testing; it is slower under real volume. We are fixing it step by step, starting with manual routing and lab fixes today.**  
2. **Customer support is overwhelmed, not because they are failing, but because order-related calls are high. Overtime and self-serve refunds are in place to help.**  
3. **If you see a shipment that could be delayed beyond 7 days, or any safety issue, use the urgent escalation path—never assume someone else will catch it.**  
4. **We expect improvements by mid-next week, but normal service is two to three weeks away. Until then, focus on what you can control: clear communication with customers and each other.**  

**Closing**  

This is a hard week. The operations team has been working 12-hour shifts. The support team is hearing frustrated customers all day. The engineering team is debugging live systems after their planned launch. I am accountable for the decision to migrate on this timeline, and I am grateful for the professionalism everyone is showing under pressure.  

If you have direct feedback about a specific process that is making your job harder, please send it to me personally. I read every message, and I will route it to the right team within 24 hours.  

Thank you for being in this with us.  

Best,  

**[Your Name]**  
COO, [Company Name]

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
Here is a story for children ages 7–10, written to your specifications.

---

**The Sparrow and the Puddle Map**

The summer rain had finally stopped. The sky was a clean, happy blue, and the whole garden smelled like wet dirt and sweetness. In a quiet corner, under a rhubarb leaf as big as an umbrella, a snail named Sable was very, very busy.

Sable was the most careful snail in the garden. He carried his home on his back, but he also carried a secret in his head: a map. It was a map of every single puddle in the entire garden. Not just the big ones, like the one by the leaking hose. He remembered the tiny, perfect ones, too—the mirror-puddle in the hollow of a stone, the long, skinny one that lived in a crack in the path.

“A puddle is a treasure,” Sable would whisper to himself as he slid his soft body along the damp earth. “But a treasure that disappears.”

He was on his way to check on the shell-puddle, a round, shallow one that looked just like his own spiral shell. It was his favorite. The raindrops had made it shimmer. He was halfway there when a tiny shadow fell across him.

*THWUMP.*

A young sparrow, all brown fluff and big, questioning eyes, landed right in the puddle Sable was heading for. Water splashed everywhere.

“Oh!” said the sparrow. Her name was Pip. “Sorry, sorry! I was trying to land on that twig. But the sky tilted and my feet forgot what to do.” She shook herself, sending a spray of droplets over Sable.

Sable didn't flinch. He was used to waiting. He waited for the sun to warm his path. He waited for the lettuce to grow. He waited for the world to slow down enough for him to cross the pavement.

“That’s a delicate puddle,” Sable said, his voice slow and smooth, like a river stone. “Now it’s all stirred up.”

Pip hopped from one foot to the other. “It’ll settle. Watch!” She flapped her wings, which were still a little clumsy. “I don’t have time for puddles! I’m learning to fly. Well, I can fly. It’s the stopping part I haven’t figured out.”

She looked up at a bobbing dandelion. “I want to fly *high*. I want to feel the wind under my belly like a giant hand, throwing me up into the blue sky!” She fluttered her wings. “But every time I try to come down, I either crash into a leaf, or tumble over a twig, or—as you just saw—land in the middle of someone’s puddle.”

“The wind is a good friend to learn from,” Sable said, leaving a tiny, silver trail behind him. “It does not rush. It pushes gently. Then it stops. It knows when to let go.”

Pip wasn’t listening. She was watching a bigger sparrow swoop from the gutter to the clothesline. “I want to do that!” she chirped. “Now!”

She launched herself into the air. Up she went, past the beans growing up their poles, past the fluttering laundry, all the way to the peak of the roof. She was a tiny brown dot against the blue. “I did it!” she cried. “I’m flying!”

But then she looked down. The ground was so far. The garden was a green and brown quilt. Her wings felt stiff. She didn't know how to turn the wind into a gentle staircase. The air rushed past her face.

“Help!” she cheeped, her voice small and scared.

She tilted her wings forward, and she plummeted. She tried to slam her feet down like brakes, but she just spun. *WHAP!* She hit a big sunflower, bounced off a watering can, and landed with a soft *plop* in the very center of the big puddle by the leaking hose. Water went up her nose.

Sable had watched the whole thing from his safe spot on a stone. He waited for her to catch her breath.

Pip climbed out, dripping and miserable. “I almost flew,” she sniffled. “But I don’t know how to land. I’m too scared.”

Sable glided over to her. “Being scared is not the opposite of being brave,” he said. “Running away from being scared is.”

Pip wiped her beak with a wing. “What do you mean?”

Sable pointed his eye-stalks towards the map in his mind. “Every day, I travel to my puddles. They get smaller. The sun drinks them. The wind licks them away. Today, the shell-puddle was here. Tomorrow, it might be gone. It takes bravery for me to visit something I know will disappear.”

“But you don’t fly,” said Pip.

“No,” said Sable. “I do something very hard. I wait. I watch the sun inch across the sky. I see the air grow cooler. Patience is my kind of bravery. It doesn't look like a dive. It looks like a ripple.”

Pip looked at the big puddle. The water had stopped shaking. It was smooth and still. It was a perfect mirror.

“Maybe,” said Sable slowly, “you could learn to land the way a puddle dries. Not all at once. A little less each time. First, you learn to fall gently. Then, you learn to stop.”

Pip tilted her head. “How does a puddle dry?” she asked.

“It cannot make the wind stop,” said Sable. “It cannot keep the sun away. So it does the bravest thing of all. It lets go, one tiny, invisible layer at a time. It becomes a smaller and smaller version of itself, until it is just a dark spot on the ground, and then—nothing. It doesn't fight. It trusts.”

Pip looked at the smooth, still water of the big puddle. She looked at Sable, who was so small and so slow, yet he had crossed the whole garden. He knew every puddle. He knew how they vanished. He knew how to wait.

She took a deep breath. “Okay,” she said.

She fluttered to a low branch of a hazel bush. It was only two feet off the ground. She didn't try to fly fast or high. She just let her wings catch the smallest bump of wind. She lifted off for one second. Then, she remembered Sable’s patience. She didn't try to stop. She let her body sink, soft and slow, like a feather, like a single raindrop falling into a puddle.

She landed on a soft clump of moss. It was a messy landing. She wobbled. But her feet held.

She looked at Sable. Her eyes were no longer big with fear. They were big with wonder.

“I landed,” she whispered.

Sable’s shell gleamed in the sun. The shell-puddle was almost gone now—just a thin, damp circle. But tomorrow there would be another rain. And another puddle. And the next time, maybe Pip would land right next to him, instead of in the middle.

He slid forward, leaving his silver trail. Patience, he knew, was the wind that never stopped, even when you couldn’t feel it. And it was the bravest thing you could carry.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Unveils New Pollinator Path Education Trail**

**HARBORLEAF, MD – July 12, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden today opened the “Pollinator Path,” a new 0.8-mile public education trail designed to help visitors understand and support local bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds. Located on the garden’s east meadow loop, the fully accessible trail features interactive learning stations, native plant demonstration beds, and tactile signage intended to translate scientific concepts into practical, at-home gardening actions.

The trail opens at a time when public interest in pollinator conservation has grown significantly. Garden staff report that visitors have increasingly requested hands-on, actionable guidance for creating pollinator-friendly spaces in their own yards, balconies, and community gardens. The Pollinator Path directly addresses this demand by offering a sequence of outdoor learning experiences that demonstrate how small, informed planting decisions can collectively strengthen regional ecosystems.

“Visitors have told us they want to help pollinators but don’t always know where to start,” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “The Pollinator Path gives them concrete, manageable steps. We show that a single pot of native milkweed on an apartment balcony or a cluster of wild bergamot in a suburban garden can make a real difference. When neighbors make those same small choices, we create connected habitats that support wildlife across entire neighborhoods.”

**Trail Features and Accessibility**

The 0.8-mile loop is paved and graded to accommodate wheelchairs, strollers, and mobility aids. Every 200 feet, the trail offers shaded resting points with benches and interpretive panels. Key features include:

- **Native Plant Demonstration Beds**: Ten curated plots showcase Pennsylvania sedge, butterfly weed, purple coneflower, and other species naturally beneficial to local pollinators. Each bed includes a companion guide explaining bloom times, host plant relationships, and soil requirements.

- **Night-Blooming Flower Station**: A dedicated twilight area highlights evening primrose, moonflower, and other nocturnal blooms that support moths and night-flying insects. Subtle low-level lighting allows evening viewing without disrupting pollinator activity.

- **Tactile Signage and Youth Observation Cards**: Braille and raised-text panels accompany each interpretive sign, providing non-visual access to trail content. Visitors ages 5–12 can pick up free observation cards that prompt them to identify pollinator species, note flower shapes, and record behavioral patterns.

- **Volunteer-Led Weekend Walks**: Beginning July 19, trained volunteer educators will lead guided walks every Saturday and Sunday at 10 a.m. and 2 p.m. These 45-minute tours focus on seasonal pollinator activity and offer personalized planting advice.

The trail’s design incorporates research from the Garden’s horticultural team, who consulted peer-reviewed studies on pollinator foraging ranges, native plant phenology, and the impact of habitat fragmentation. The east meadow location was chosen for its existing diversity of wildflowers and its proximity to the Garden’s main entrance, allowing easy access for school groups and families.

**Educational Goals and Community Impact**

Harborleaf Botanical Garden emphasizes that the Pollinator Path is a nonpolitical, science-based educational resource. The trail does not advocate legislation or endorse specific products. Instead, it focuses on ecological principles: plants that provide nectar and larval host material, the importance of bloom succession from early spring to late fall, and the role of pesticide-free gardening practices.

Ortiz noted that the trail’s content is designed to be inclusive of all experience levels. “Whether you are a master gardener or someone who has never planted a seed, you will find something useful here,” she said. “Our goal is not to overwhelm, but to inspire. Every sign, every planting bed, every observation card is a conversation starter about how we share our landscapes with the creatures that keep our food systems and ecosystems healthy.”

The Garden plans to update the trail’s interpretive materials annually to reflect changes in pollinator populations and emerging research. Volunteers will rotate observation station activities each season, including spring “nest-spotting” for solitary bees and fall “seed harvest” demonstrations.

**Opening Day Events**

To mark the opening, the Garden is hosting a family-friendly event from 9 a.m. to 4 p.m. on July 12. Activities include a guided scavenger hunt along the trail, a native plant “swap table” for visitors to exchange seeds and cuttings, and a pollinator-themed story time for young children at 11 a.m. and 1 p.m. Admission to the Garden is free for members and included with regular daytime admission for non-members.

The Pollinator Path is now open daily from dawn to dusk. Weekend volunteer-led walks begin July 19; no advance registration is required.

**About Harborleaf Botanical Garden**

Harborleaf Botanical Garden is a public institution dedicated to plant conservation, education, and ecological stewardship. The Garden manages more than 40 acres of curated landscapes, including woodlands, wetlands, and restored meadows, and serves as a research site for native plant ecology. Its learning programs reach more than 15,000 students and community members annually through school field trips, adult workshops, and public events.

# # #

**Media Contact:**
Elena Vasquez
Communications Coordinator
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
1200 Meadow Lane
Harborleaf, MD 21015
Phone: (410) 555-0198
Email: evasquez@harborleafgarden.org
Website: www.harborleafgarden.org/pollinatorpath

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The laundromat on Harbor Street smelled of bleach and salt, even in the dark. The power had cut twenty minutes ago, plunging the small coastal town into a hush that felt older than the flickering cell phones and the single battery lantern someone had set on the folding counter.

Maya had been leaning against a dryers, watching her towels slosh to a stop in the dimming light. A half-finished load. She’d been about to stuff them, damp and useless, into her bag when the door chimed and a man stepped in, holding the lantern.

He was taller than she remembered. His jaw had squared, his hair was shorter at the sides, and he wore a waxed jacket that looked too new for this town. But the way he hesitated in the doorway—the slight cock of his head as he registered the machines, the wet air, the silhouette by the window—that was all Leo.

“Oh,” he said. The single syllable hung in the space between them.

Maya’s hand tightened on the plastic bag of wet towels. “You’re back.”

“Just for the week.” He set the lantern on the folding counter, careful, like it might break. Its light pushed soft amber across the linoleum, catching the salt-frosted glass of the window. “My aunt’s selling the house. I came to help clear it out.” A pause. “I didn’t know you were still here.”

She wasn’t sure what she felt. It came as a pressure behind her ribs, not sharp, but steady. “I never left.”

The words sat in the quiet. A washing machine clicked, trying to restart, and failed.

Leo moved the lantern closer to her, an unconscious gesture she remembered—he used to slide his coffee toward her side of the porch step, just to make sure she had warmth. “I saw the light from the street,” he said, filling the silence with the simple truth. “I thought someone might be stranded.”

Maya almost smiled. He’d always been like that. Looking for the stranded. “I’m fine. Just towels.” She gestured at the dark machine. “They’ll survive until morning.”

He nodded, but he didn’t leave. He leaned against the next dryer over, keeping a polite distance. The lantern sat between them like a small, steady sun.

The last time she’d seen him, it had been August. The air was thick with heat and the smell of sun-warmed pavement. He’d said he was driving to the city for a weekend, just to look at a program. She’d told him to bring back good coffee. He’d laughed and said he’d text her the menu.

He never texted. And on Monday, the For Sale sign went up in his aunt’s yard. Maya had stood on her own porch and watched a moving truck swallow the furniture, the porch swing, the stack of paint cans they’d left on the steps after a half-hearted mural project. She’d waited for a call, a note, anything. But the silence held.

“I still have your voicemail,” she said now.

He looked up quickly. His expression was hard to read in the low light—something between surprise and a flinch. “Which one?”

“The one from that Thursday. You called to ask if I wanted dinner from the Clam Shack.” She pushed the wet towels aside and pulled out her phone. The screen glowed as she scrolled. “It’s the only one I never deleted.”

She held it out. The voicemail icon sat there, unplayed for years but never erased. A piece of evidence. A paperweight on memory.

Leo looked at it, then at her. His voice went quieter. “Maya.”

“Six years,” she said. “I thought maybe you’d died. Or hated me. Or both.”

“I didn’t hate you.”

“You left without a goodbye.”

The words hit the linoleum like dropped coins. He let them lie. His jaw worked, and he looked down at his hands—hands she’d watched paint a fence, knot a fishing line, hold a coffee cup with more steadiness than they seemed to have now.

“I was scared,” he said finally. “I wasn’t sure I’d come back. And I didn’t know how to say that without—without making it sound like you were the reason I needed to leave.” He stopped. Rubbed his palm over his face. “That’s not better, is it?”

“It’s something.”

“You deserved a goodbye.” The words came slower, each one deliberate. “You deserved a lot more than a voicemail about fried clams.”

Maya looked away. Out the window, the street was dark except for a single porch light two blocks down. The tide was low; she could smell the exposed mudflats, the brine and the cold.

“I used to imagine what I’d say if you showed up,” she said. “I had whole speeches. Accusations. Questions. Then I got tired of keeping them polished.”

“Do you still have them?”

“No. I just have the voicemail.”

He smiled then, just a little. It wasn’t a happy smile, but it was real. “You could delete it now.”

She looked at the phone, then back at him. “Maybe I’ll keep it. Remind me that people sometimes call back.”

He pulled in a breath. The silence stretched, not uncomfortable, but full of all the years they hadn’t spoken. The lantern glowed. The washing machine sat dark.

Maya lifted the bag of wet towels. “The power’s not coming back tonight. I should go.”

She reached for the lantern, then stopped. It wasn’t hers. She left it where it was.

At the door, his voice stopped her.

“Maya.”

She turned. He was still leaning against the dryer, the lantern at his elbow. His face was half-lit, half-shadow, the same geometry she remembered from a thousand summer evenings.

“I’m sorry,” he said.

Two words. Barely audible. Heavy.

She held his gaze for a long moment. Her hand rested on the door handle. The night air slipped in, cool and wet.

“I heard you,” she said. Quiet. Not forgiveness. Not refusal. Just acknowledgment.

She stepped outside. The door sighed shut behind her.

Alone in the laundromat, Leo stood in the amber light. He didn’t move to follow. He looked at her phone’s absence, at the empty space by the window where she’d been, and then down at the dark machine full of towels that would never spin dry tonight.

He pulled out his own phone. Opened a new voicemail. Set it on the counter, recording.

The silence was long. Then his voice, barely above a whisper:

“I’m at the old laundromat. I’ll be here until the power comes back. In case you want your towels.”

He ended it. Did not send. Held the phone in his palm.

Outside, Maya stood in the dark street. She could see the lantern glow through the laundromat’s front window, a warm smudge in the black.

She did not walk back inside.

But she did not walk away, either.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# Throttle with Purpose: Designing Rate Limits for a Multi-Tenant API

Rate limiting is one of those topics that seems simple until you have to make it work for both a five-person startup and a Fortune 500 enterprise sharing the same API. Get it wrong, and you either crush legitimate traffic or let one noisy tenant degrade everyone’s experience. This article walks through the core concepts, algorithm trade-offs, tenant isolation strategies, client-facing signals, and observability needs for a multi-tenant backend.

## 1. Authentication, Authorization, Quotas, and Rate Limits – The Four Layers

Before designing limits, be absolutely clear about what each mechanism does and where it fits in the request lifecycle.

**Authentication** answers “Who are you?” It validates credentials (API keys, OAuth tokens) and establishes identity. Without authentication, you cannot apply per-tenant or per-user limits.

**Authorization** answers “What are you allowed to do?” It checks permissions after authentication: can this tenant create resources? Can this user read sensitive data? Rate limits are not a substitute for authorization.

**Quotas** define *hard boundaries* on consumption over a long period – 1,000 API calls per month, 10 GB of storage, or 100 concurrent active projects. Quotas are enforced asynchronously or at the start of a billing cycle; they protect business models, not system stability.

**Rate limits** protect *operational capacity* over short time windows – seconds to minutes. They prevent a single misbehaving client (or a coordinated burst) from exhausting CPU, database connections, or memory. Rate limits are the first line of defense for reliability; quotas are the second line for business logic.

The key insight: rate limits should never be used to enforce a contract. If a customer agreed to 10,000 calls/hour, you honor that. Rate limits kick in only when bursts threaten the system’s ability to serve *any* tenant.

## 2. Token Bucket vs. Fixed Window vs. Sliding Window – Practical Trade-Offs

Three algorithms dominate production rate limiters. Here is how they behave under load.

**Fixed Window** divides time into discrete buckets (e.g., one per minute). Each bucket has a counter that resets at the top of the minute. It is simple to implement and cheap in memory. However, it suffers from the “border burst” problem: if a client sends 100 requests at 11:59:59 and 100 requests at 12:00:01, the system sees 200 requests in two seconds but only 100 in each window. This defeats the purpose of short-term protection.

**Sliding Window** solves the border burst by tracking timestamps per request (or per increment). A rolling window of, say, 60 seconds counts requests from the current time backward. Implementations range from storing every timestamp (accurate but memory-heavy) to tracking a counter plus the previous window’s count (a compromise known as sliding window counter). Sliding windows feel fairer to clients—no sudden reset—but require more coordination in distributed systems.

**Token Bucket** is the workhorse of production systems. It works by refilling a bucket of tokens at a steady rate (e.g., 10 tokens per second) up to a maximum burst capacity (e.g., 100 tokens). A client consumes one token per request; if no tokens remain, the request is delayed or rejected. Token buckets naturally allow short bursts (up to the bucket size) while enforcing a long-term average. They are intuitive for engineers and map well to real-world capacity planning—the refill rate is your steady-state throughput, and the bucket size is your tolerance for spikes.

**Recommendation**: Use token bucket for most multi-tenant APIs. It provides burst tolerance (good for small teams that occasionally need to process a batch) and a hard average limit (essential for enterprise tenants that share infrastructure). Pair it with a sliding window counter only if you need precise per-second fairness and can afford the memory overhead.

## 3. Tenant Isolation – Never Punish One Tenant for Another’s Noisy Integration

The simplest mistake is a global counter shared across all tenants. A single misconfigured integration on one team’s account can exhaust the global token pool, blocking every other tenant. That is unacceptable.

**Per-tenant buckets** are the minimum: each API key or tenant ID gets its own token bucket. Memory scales linearly with tenant count, which is manageable for thousands of tenants but not millions. For large-scale systems, use a sharded Redis cluster with a token bucket per tenant key, or move to a tiered approach where “small” tenants share a bucket (with individual sub-limits) and “enterprise” tenants get dedicated resources.

**Hierarchical rate limits** add another layer. Apply a global cluster-level limit (e.g., total requests per second across all tenants) to protect the infrastructure, then per-tenant limits that are a fraction of the global capacity. Overcommit slightly: the sum of per-tenant limits can exceed global capacity, because not all tenants burst simultaneously. But never let one tenant consume more than 25–30% of global capacity without manual review. This prevents a noisy neighbor from starving everyone else.

**Integration-level sub-limits** prevent a tenant from shooting itself in the foot. A tenant might have 1,000 requests/second total, but a single long-polling connection or a retry loop can saturate that. Offer the ability to set per-integration (per-API key) or per-endpoint limits inside the tenant’s bucket. This moves isolation from “tenant vs. everyone” to “integration vs. same tenant other integrations.”

## 4. Headers and Error Responses – Tell Clients Exactly What Happened

Clients cannot behave well if they have no feedback. Return these headers on every response:

- `X-RateLimit-Limit`: the maximum number of requests allowed in the current window (or bucket capacity).
- `X-RateLimit-Remaining`: how many requests remain.
- `X-RateLimit-Reset`: a Unix timestamp (or ISO 8601) indicating when the counter resets or the bucket refills.

When a request is rejected, use HTTP 429 Too Many Requests. The body should include:

- A human-readable message: “Rate limit exceeded. Retry after [time].”
- A `Retry-After` header with the number of seconds the client should wait (integer or HTTP-date).
- Optionally, a structured JSON body with fields like `error.code`, `error.message`, and `error.retry_after_seconds`.

**Critical**: Distinguish between rate limits (operational) and quota limits (contractual). A 429 with “rate limit exceeded” means back off for seconds. A 403 or 429 with “quota exceeded” means you have hit your monthly cap and need to upgrade or wait for the billing cycle. Use different HTTP status codes or distinct error codes so automation can react appropriately.

## 5. Observability – Are Your Limits Doing Their Job?

Rate limits should block bad behavior, not hurt good customers. Metrics and alerts are the only way to know.

**Metrics to track**:
- `rate_limit.requests_total` by tenant tier, endpoint, and status (allowed / blocked).
- `rate_limit.blocked_requests_total` with labels for tenant ID and integration key.
- `rate_limit.throttled_requests_total` for requests allowed after waiting (if you use queuing).
- `rate_limit.latency_ms` for the rate limiter itself – it must be fast (sub-millisecond).
- `rate_limit.token_refill_rate` and `rate_limit.bucket_capacity` per tier.

**Logs**: Log every blocked request at warning level. Include tenant ID, endpoint, current usage count, limit, and the algorithm in use. Do not log every allowed request—that creates noise equal to total traffic.

**Alerts**:
1. **Global traffic spike** – if total requests per second exceeds 80% of global bucket capacity for > 1 minute, alert the on-call.
2. **Per-tenant throttling spike** – if a single tenant experiences > 10% blocked requests over 5 minutes, investigate. They may have a bug or need a plan upgrade.
3. **Rate limit latency** – if the rate limiter itself takes > 5ms p99, it is becoming a bottleneck.
4. **False positive detection** – if a tenant that rarely hits limits suddenly gets blocked extensively, check for misconfiguration (e.g., too aggressive a sub-limit on a new integration).

## Recommended Baseline Design

For a multi-tenant API serving both small teams and enterprises:

- **Algorithm**: Per-tenant token bucket with 1-second refill granularity. Bucket capacity = 150% of the refill rate per second to allow small bursts.
- **Implementation**: Centralized Redis cluster with key format `ratelimit:{tenant_id}:{bucket_name}`. Use a Lua script to atomically check and decrement tokens. This avoids race conditions and is fast (< 1ms).
- **Tiers**: Define three tiers: “Starter” (50 req/s, burst 75), “Pro” (500 req/s, burst 750), “Enterprise” (5000 req/s, burst 7500 or custom). Overcommit global capacity by 2x.
- **Isolation**: Each tenant gets its own bucket. Offer optional sub-buckets per API key inside the tenant’s account.
- **Headers**: Return `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset` on every response. Use 429 + `Retry-After` on rejection.
- **Observability**: Counters for requests and blocks broken by tenant tier and endpoint. Alerts for >10% per-tenant blocking rate and >80% global capacity utilization.

This design balances fairness, performance, and simplicity. It gives small teams the burst they need for batch jobs and gives enterprises the isolation they demand—without letting one tenant’s loop bring down the entire system.

messages.content:
请帮我写一个单文件 HTML 水族箱动画。

要求：
1. 画面里有多条不同颜色、不同大小的鱼，游动要尽量自然。
2. 用户左键点击水族箱时，可以在点击位置放下一粒鱼食。
3. 每条鱼会追逐离自己最近的鱼食并尝试吃掉它；没有鱼食时恢复自由游动。
4. 需要有完整的 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript，能直接保存为一个 `.html` 文件运行。
5. 视觉效果要精致一些，不要只给伪代码或解释，直接给出完整代码。

请帮我创建一个单文件 HTML 时钟应用，要求直接给出完整代码。

功能和视觉要求：
1. 时钟表盘是圆形，有刻度、数字、时针、分针、秒针，并在表盘内部显示当前时间和日期。
2. 表盘需要有简洁的高级感：白色背景、深色边框、轻微 3D 阴影。
3. 用 JavaScript 动态生成 60 个刻度，整点刻度更粗更深。
4. 时间必须转换为中国标准时间（北京时区，Asia/Shanghai）。
5. 写一个 updateClock() 函数，计算时针、分针、秒针角度；秒针需要尽量平滑运动。
6. 使用 setInterval 或 requestAnimationFrame 驱动刷新。
7. 代码需要兼容 Edge 浏览器。

请将下面的中文商务写作材料翻译成英文。要求：面向现代职场读者，语气清楚、专业、克制；不要翻译成生硬腔调；保留作者关于“商务信函应传达信息并建立善意”的核心观点。

素材来源：根据公共领域英文书《The Book of Business Etiquette》相关章节改写的中文素材
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists the source eBook as public domain in the USA; this Chinese passage is a benchmark rewrite.

【待处理素材】
许多商务信函本不必写，而已经写出的信中，又有不少因为信息残缺、结构混乱，使原本两封信便能完成并归档的交易，拖成六封或八封往返。好的信件来自清楚的思考和周密的安排。销售函有时需要数周打磨；普通业务往来只需几分钟也足够。关键不在于花了多久，而在于最终写出的信是否能准确完成任务。

衡量商务信函的规则很多，但真正不可缺少的只有两点：第一，信必须让收件人明白你的意思；第二，它必须为公司建立善意。最好的信，是用最简短、最礼貌、最完整的方式做到这两点。每封信背后都应有明确理由，哪怕只是向客户说一声谢谢。许多精力浪费在无目的的广告、无效的推销和多余的来信上；这些文字占用了打字机和纸张，却没有推动任何事情前进。

一封好信不应让读者注意它本身的文采，而应让读者接收到它要传达的消息。某出版社曾寄出一批推销新书的测试信，回信者称赞信写得漂亮，却无人订购图书。这说明文字若抢走了信息的焦点，再优雅也不是成功的商务写作。信纸、信头、版式、段落都应整洁、克制、清楚；过分花哨的设计和故作新奇的格式，只会让人分心。

商务写作还应尊重读者的时间。清楚的标题、适当的称呼、按主题分段的正文、明确的请求和完整的联系方式，往往比华丽措辞更能建立信任。若一封信需要收件人回复，写信者应说明希望对方在何时以前回复、需要提供哪些信息，以及下一步将如何处理。这样，信件不仅传递态度，也推动事务向前。

message.content:
Many business letters need never have been written; and of those that are written, a great many suffer from incomplete information or poor organization, turning what could have been resolved in two exchanged letters into six or eight rounds of correspondence. Good letters come from clear thinking and careful planning. A sales letter may sometimes require weeks of refinement; routine business correspondence can be done in a few minutes. What matters is not how long it takes, but whether the final letter accomplishes its task accurately.

There are many rules for evaluating a business letter, but only two are truly indispensable: first, the letter must make your meaning clear to the recipient; second, it must build goodwill for your company. The best letters achieve both in the shortest, most courteous, and most complete manner. Every letter should have a clear reason behind it, even if it is simply to say thank you to a customer. Too much effort is wasted on aimless advertisements, ineffective solicitations, and unnecessary correspondence—words that consume paper and typewriter time without moving anything forward.

A good letter should not draw attention to its own style; rather, it should let the reader receive the message it conveys. A publishing house once sent out a batch of test letters promoting a new book. Recipients praised the letters as beautifully written, but no one placed an order. This shows that when the language steals focus from the message, even the most elegant writing is not successful business communication. Stationery, letterhead, layout, and paragraphs should all be neat, restrained, and clear. Overly elaborate design or forced novelty in format only distracts.

Business writing should also respect the reader’s time. Clear subject lines, appropriate salutations, paragraphs organized by topic, explicit requests, and complete contact information do far more to build trust than ornate phrasing. If a letter requires a reply, the writer should indicate by when a response is needed, what information is required, and what the next steps will be. In this way, the letter not only conveys attitude but also moves the matter forward.

请将下面的繁体中文寓言/哲理片段翻译成英文，目标读者为青少年读者。要求：保留想象力和节奏；遇到典故不要长篇解释；译文应清楚、流畅，适合作为儿童文学或寓言文本阅读。

素材来源：《莊子的故事》节选：逍遥游开篇
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23913
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
北冥有魚，其名為鯤。鯤之大，不知其幾千里也。化而為鳥，其名為鵬。鵬之背，不知其幾千里也；怒而飛，其翼若垂天之雲。是鳥也，海運則將徙於南冥。南冥者，天池也。齊諧者，志怪者也。諧之言曰：「鵬之徙於南冥也，水擊三千里，摶扶搖而上者九萬里，去以六月息者也。」野馬也，塵埃也，生物之以息相吹也。天之蒼蒼，其正色邪？其遠而無所至極邪？其視下也，亦若是則已矣。且夫水之積也不厚，則其負大舟也無力。覆杯水於坳堂之上，則芥為之舟；置杯焉則膠，水淺而舟大也。風之積也不厚，則其負大翼也無力。故九萬里，則風斯在下矣，而後乃今掊風；背負青天而莫之夭閼者，而後乃今將圖南。蜩與學鳩笑之曰：「我決起而飛，槍榆枋而止，時則不至而控於地而已矣，奚以這九萬里而南為？」適莽蒼者，三餐而反，腹猶果然；適百里者，宿舂糧；適千里者，三月聚糧。之二蟲又何知！小知不及大知，小年不及大年。奚以知其然也？朝菌不知晦朔，蟪蛄不知春秋，此小年也。楚之南有冥靈者，以五百歲為春，五百歲為秋；上古有大椿者，以八千歲為春，八千歲為秋。此大年也。而彭祖乃今以久特聞，眾人匹之，不亦悲乎！湯之問棘也是已。窮髮之北有冥海者，天池也。有魚焉，其廣數千里，未有知其脩者，其名為鯤。有鳥焉，其名為鵬，背若泰山，翼若垂天之雲，摶扶搖羊角而上者九萬里，絕雲氣，負青天，然後圖南，且適南冥也。斥鴳笑之曰：「彼且奚適也？我騰躍而上，不過數仞而下，翱翔蓬蒿之間，此亦飛之至也，而彼且奚適也？」此小大之辯也。故夫知效一官，行比一鄉，德合一君，而徵一國者，其自視也亦若此矣。而宋榮子猶然笑之。且舉世而譽之而不加勸，舉世而非之而不加沮，定乎內外之分，辯乎榮辱之竟，斯已矣。彼其於世，未數數然也。雖然，猶有未樹也。夫列子御風而行，泠然善也，旬有五日而反。彼於致福者，未數數然也。此雖免乎行，猶有所待者也。若夫乘天地之正，而御六氣之辯，以遊無窮者，彼且惡乎待哉！故曰：至人無己，神人無功，聖人無名。

message.content:
Here is the English translation of the excerpt from *Zhuangzi's Stories* (the beginning of "The Free and Easy Wandering"), adapted for young readers. The translation preserves the imaginative tone, rhythm, and key ideas without over-explaining cultural references.

---

In the Northern Ocean, there is a fish. Its name is Kun. The Kun is so huge—no one knows how many thousand miles wide it is. It transforms into a bird. Its name is Peng. The Peng’s back—no one knows how many thousand miles across it is. When it rouses itself and flies, its wings are like clouds hanging from the sky.

This bird, when the seas stir, sets out to journey to the Southern Ocean. The Southern Ocean is the Heavenly Pool.

There is a book called *Qi Xie*, a collection of strange tales. It says: “When the Peng travels to the Southern Ocean, it stirs the water for three thousand miles, and rides the whirlwind up to ninety thousand miles. It leaves on the breath of the sixth month.”

Like wild horses galloping, like dust blowing—these are living things, moved by the breath of the air. The sky is deep blue—is that its true color? Or is it so far away that there is no end to it? When the Peng looks down from above, it must seem no different to it than the sky seems to us.

Now, if water is not deep enough, it cannot hold up a great ship. Pour a cup of water into a hollow on the floor—a mustard seed can float on it like a boat. But set a cup there, and it sticks fast—the water is too shallow, and the boat is too big. In the same way, if the wind is not deep enough, it cannot support great wings. So the Peng must rise ninety thousand miles—only then is the wind beneath it. Then, riding the wind, carrying the blue sky on its back, nothing can block or hinder it—and only then can it set its course for the south.

The cicada and the little dove laughed at the Peng. “We take off with a flutter,” they said. “We stop at the elm or the sandalwood tree. Sometimes we don’t make it, and we just drop to the ground. What’s the use of going ninety thousand miles to the south?”

If you go to the nearby woods, you take three meals and come back—still full. If you travel a hundred miles, you grind grain for a night’s journey. If you travel a thousand miles, you store up food for three months. What do those two little creatures know?

Small knowledge cannot compare to great knowledge. A short life cannot compare to a long life. How do we know this? The morning mushroom does not know the end of the month. The cicada does not know spring and autumn. That is the short-lived life. In the south of Chu, there is a tree called the Mingling. Five hundred years are its spring, and five hundred years its autumn. In ancient times, there was a great Chun tree, with eight thousand years as its spring and eight thousand years as its autumn. That is the long-lived life. And yet Pengzu is famous for his long life, and everyone wants to match him. Isn’t that sad?

King Tang asked his wise minister Ji the same question. In the barren north, there is a dark sea—the Heavenly Pool. In it lives a fish, several thousand miles wide—no one knows how long. Its name is Kun. There is a bird named Peng, with a back like Mount Tai, and wings like clouds hanging from the sky. It rides the whirlwind, spiraling up ninety thousand miles, piercing the clouds, carrying the blue sky on its back—and then it turns south, heading for the Southern Ocean.

The little quail laughed at it. “Where does it think it’s going?” it said. “I hop up and down, fly a few yards, and then drop into the bushes. That’s as good as flying gets! Where does it think it’s going?”

This is the difference between the small and the great.

So it is with people: some are smart enough for one small office, some good enough for one village, some worthy enough for one ruler, some capable enough for one whole kingdom. They look at themselves just like the little quail.

But Master Song Rong just laughed at them. Even if the whole world praised him, he wouldn’t try harder. Even if the whole world blamed him, he wouldn’t feel discouraged. He knew the difference between inside and outside, and he understood where honor and disgrace truly lay. That was all. He didn’t fret about the world. And yet, even he hadn’t reached the highest level.

Then there was Liezi, who could ride the wind and travel, light and graceful. He would return after fifteen days. He didn’t bother much about happiness either. But even though he didn’t have to walk, he still depended on something—on the wind.

But the one who rides the true nature of heaven and earth, who masters the six changes of the seasons, and wanders through the infinite—what does that one need to depend on?

So it is said: The Perfect Man has no self. The Spirit Man has no achievements. The Sage has no name.
