Let me see: that would be four thousand miles down, I think—” (for, you see, Alice had learnt several things of this sort in her lessons in the schoolroom, and though this was not a _very_ good opportunity for showing off her knowledge, as there was no one to listen to her, still it was good practice to say it over) “—yes, that’s about the right distance—but then I wonder what Latitude or Longitude I’ve got to?” (Alice had no idea what Latitude was, or Longitude either, but thought they were nice grand words to say.)

message.content:
爱丽丝坐在姐姐身旁的河岸上，无聊得要命——没事可干，闷得慌。她偷偷朝姐姐读的那本书瞅了一两眼，可那上面既没有图画，也没有对话。“一本书，”爱丽丝心想，“要是既没有图画又没有对话，那还有什么意思呢？”

她在心里琢磨着（说“琢磨”也不太确切，因为炎热的天气让她昏昏沉沉、迷迷糊糊的），编一条雏菊花环的乐趣，值不值得她费劲起身去摘那些雏菊呢？就在这时，一只粉红眼睛的白兔突然从她身边跑过。

本来这也没什么好大惊小怪的，而且听见兔子自言自语“哎呀！哎呀！我要迟到了！”时，爱丽丝起初也没觉得这有什么不对劲（后来她回想起来，才觉得当时自己本该感到奇怪，可那一瞬间一切都显得那么自然）。可那只兔子竟然——竟然从它的背心口袋里掏出一块怀表，看了一眼，又匆匆赶路了。爱丽丝一下子跳了起来，因为她突然想到：自己从来没见过兔子穿背心，更没见过兔子从背心口袋里掏怀表。一股强烈的好奇心燃起来，她撒腿就追，刚好赶在兔子钻进篱笆底下一个大兔子洞之前瞥见了它的身影。

紧接着，爱丽丝也跟着跳进了兔子洞，压根儿没想过自己究竟还能不能出来。

那兔子洞起初像条隧道，笔直延伸了一段，然后猛然向下拐去——拐得那么突然，爱丽丝还没反应过来该怎么停住，就已经掉进了一口深不见底的井里。

要么是井太深，要么是她落得太慢，总之，她往下坠的时候有足够的时间东张西望，想想接下来会发生什么。她先是吃力地往下看，想弄清自己会落到什么地方，可底下太黑，什么也看不见；接着她又打量四周的井壁，发现上面摆满了碗柜和书架，这儿那儿还用钉子挂着地图和图画。她经过一个架子时顺手拿下一个罐子，标签上写着“橘子果酱”，可让她大失所望的是——罐子是空的。她没敢把罐子扔掉，怕砸到下面的人，于是尽量在往下落经过一个碗柜时，又把它放了回去。

“好呀！”爱丽丝心想，“经过这么一次摔落，以后滚下楼梯可就根本不算回事啦！家里人准会觉得我多勇敢啊！哼，就算我从房顶上摔下来，我也一个字都不会吭！”（这倒很有可能是真的。）

往下掉，往下掉，往下掉。这摔落难道永远没有尽头了吗？“不知道到现在为止我掉了多少英里啦？”她大声说，“我准是快到地球中心了。让我想想：那应该是往下四千里左右——”要知道，爱丽丝在学校课堂上学过不少这类知识，虽然眼下没人听她说，不是卖弄学问的好机会，但能把这些念叨一遍也算是个不错的练习——“没错，差不多就是这个距离——可我现在到了什么纬度或者经度呢？”（爱丽丝其实根本不懂纬度和经度是什么，只是觉得这两个词听起来挺气派的。）

messages.content:
Translate the following English news-release excerpt into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: accurate, natural, suitable for a general news audience; keep the institutional name as “美国地质调查局（USGS）” on first mention; do not add facts or commentary.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey news release: New USGS diagram re-envisions how Earth’s water cycles the planet
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/news/national-news-release/new-usgs-diagram-re-envisions-how-earths-most-precious-commodity-cycles
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
RESTON, Va.— Starting today, educators around the nation will have a more accurate and more comprehensive tool to explain the Earth’s water cycle with the unveiling of the new U.S. Geological Survey water cycle diagram.

The revised version replaces one used by hundreds of thousands of educators and students internationally every year since 2000. So why the new water cycle? This depiction brings humans into the picture, showing the water cycle as a complex interplay of small, interconnected cycles that people interact with and influence, rather than one big circle.

USGS experts consulted with more than 100 educators and more than 30 hydrologic experts to develop the new diagram. The vast amounts of water data that USGS has collected in recent decades has informed a nuanced perspective of the water cycle, demonstrating how both its human and natural components are interconnected.

Where the existing water cycle diagram depicted only the natural aspects of the cycle, the new version depicts how Earth’s water moves and is stored, both naturally and because of human actions. Not only does the new diagram illustrate a more comprehensive view of the water cycle, it draws on principles of information design to focus attention on the water as it moves through the natural and built environment. It shows how multiple ecosystems—including a coastal plain, dry basin, wet basin, and agricultural basin—are connected across watersheds and at continental scales.

The new diagram will initially be available in both English and Spanish, with the expectation it will be translated into many other languages by end users, as was the previous version.

message.content:
弗吉尼亚州雷斯顿 — 自即日起，全美教育工作者将拥有一个更精确、更全面的工具来解释地球水循环——随着美国地质调查局（USGS）新版水循环示意图的发布。

此次修订版本取代了自2000年以来每年被国内外数十万教育工作者和学生使用的旧版示意图。为何要推出新版水循环图？该图将人类活动纳入其中，展示水循环并非单一的大循环，而是由多个相互关联的微型循环构成的复杂系统，人类与这些循环相互作用并对其产生影响。

美国地质调查局（USGS）专家在编制新版示意图时，咨询了超过100名教育工作者和30多位水文学专家。该机构近几十年来收集的大量水资源数据，为其提供了对水循环的细致视角，揭示了人类与自然组成部分如何相互关联。

旧版水循环示意图仅描绘了自然循环的方面，而新版则展示了地球水资源如何因自然过程和人类活动而发生迁移与储存。新版示意图不仅呈现了更全面的水循环视图，还借鉴了信息设计原则，聚焦于水在自然环境和人造环境中的流动过程。它展示了多个生态系统——包括沿海平原、干旱盆地、湿润盆地和农业盆地——如何跨越流域并在大陆尺度上彼此连接。

新版示意图初始将以英语和西班牙语提供，预计将由最终用户翻译成其他多种语言，正如旧版示意图的翻译情况一样。

messages.content:
Translate the following English literary passage into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: preserve emotional tension, social nuance, and indirect narration; do not make the style slangy; names may be translated conventionally or kept in English consistently. Do not add explanations.

素材来源：Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice, Chapter XXXIV excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1342
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When they were gone, Elizabeth, as if intending to exasperate herself as much as possible against Mr. Darcy, chose for her employment the examination of all the letters which Jane had written to her since her being in Kent. They contained no actual complaint, nor was there any revival of past occurrences, or any communication of present suffering. But in all, and in almost every line of each, there was a want of that cheerfulness which had been used to characterize her style, and which, proceeding from the serenity of a mind at ease with itself, and kindly disposed towards everyone, had been scarcely ever clouded. Elizabeth noticed every sentence conveying the idea of uneasiness, with an attention which it had hardly received on the first perusal. Mr. Darcy’s shameful boast of what misery he had been able to inflict gave her a keener sense of her sister’s sufferings. It was some consolation to think that his visit to Rosings was to end on the day after the next, and a still greater that in less than a fortnight she should herself be with Jane again, and enabled to contribute to the recovery of her spirits, by all that affection could do.

She could not think of Darcy’s leaving Kent without remembering that his cousin was to go with him; but Colonel Fitzwilliam had made it clear that he had no intentions at all, and, agreeable as he was, she did not mean to be unhappy about him.

While settling this point, she was suddenly roused by the sound of the door-bell; and her spirits were a little fluttered by the idea of its being Colonel Fitzwilliam himself, who had once before called late in the evening, and might now come to inquire particularly after her. But this idea was soon banished, and her spirits were very differently affected, when, to her utter amazement, she saw Mr. Darcy walk into the room. In a hurried manner he immediately began an inquiry after her health, imputing his visit to a wish of hearing that she were better. She answered him with cold civility. He sat down for a few moments, and then getting up walked about the room. Elizabeth was surprised, but said not a word. After a silence of several minutes, he came towards her in an agitated manner, and thus began:--

“In vain have I struggled. It will not do. My feelings will not be repressed. You must allow me to tell you how ardently I admire and love you.”

Elizabeth’s astonishment was beyond expression. She stared, coloured, doubted, and was silent. This he considered sufficient encouragement, and the avowal of all that he felt and had long felt for her immediately followed. He spoke well; but there were feelings besides those of the heart to be detailed, and he was not more eloquent on the subject of tenderness than of pride. His sense of her inferiority, of its being a degradation, of the family obstacles which judgment had always opposed to inclination, were dwelt on with a warmth which seemed due to the consequence he was wounding, but was very unlikely to recommend his suit.

In spite of her deeply-rooted dislike, she could not be insensible to the compliment of such a man’s affection, and though her intentions did not vary for an instant, she was at first sorry for the pain he was to receive; till roused to resentment by his subsequent language, she lost all compassion in anger. She tried, however, to compose herself to answer him with patience, when he should have done. He concluded with representing to her the strength of that attachment which in spite of all his endeavours he had found impossible to conquer; and with expressing his hope that it would now be rewarded by her acceptance of his hand. As he said this she could easily see that he had no doubt of a favourable answer. He _spoke_ of apprehension and anxiety, but his countenance expressed real security. Such a circumstance could only exasperate farther; and when he ceased the colour rose into her cheeks and she said,--

“In such cases as this, it is, I believe, the established mode to express a sense of obligation for the sentiments avowed, however unequally they may be returned. It is natural that obligation should be felt, and if I could _feel_ gratitude, I would now thank you. But I cannot--I have never desired your good opinion, and you have certainly bestowed it most unwillingly. I am sorry to have occasioned pain to anyone. It has been most unconsciously done, however, and I hope will be of short duration. The feelings which you tell me have long prevented the acknowledgment of your regard can have little difficulty in overcoming it after this explanation.”

message.content:
他们走后，伊丽莎白仿佛有意要尽可能激起自己对达西先生的厌恶，便挑出简从肯特寄来的全部信件仔细阅读。信中并无切实的怨言，既未重提旧事，也未倾诉眼下的苦楚。但每封信的几乎每一行，都失却了惯常那种欢快神采——那原是她文风的标志，源自内心宁静的从容和对所有人的善意，几乎从未被阴翳笼罩。伊丽莎白此刻留意到每一句暗示不安的措辞，其专注程度远超初读之时。达西先生洋洋自得地夸耀自己造成了何等痛苦，这让她对姐姐所受的折磨体会得更加深切。想到他后天就要离开罗辛斯，多少算是一丝慰藉；而更令她欣慰的是，不到两周自己就能回到简身边，用全部亲情所能给予的一切，帮助她重振精神。

一想到达西要离开肯特，她就不由记起他的表兄也将同行。不过费茨威廉上校早已表明自己并无此意；尽管他讨人喜欢，她也不打算为他伤神。

正想到这里，门铃声突然惊扰了她；她的心微微一跳，以为是费茨威廉上校本人来了——他曾有一次深夜来访，现在或许又是专程来问候她的。但这个念头很快被打消了，而当她万分惊愕地看见达西先生走进房间时，心境顿时起了截然不同的变化。他进门便急急询问她的健康状况，声称自己来访是希望听到她好转的消息。她答以冷淡的客套。他坐了片刻，随即起身在房间里踱步。伊丽莎白虽感意外，却一言不发。沉默了几分钟后，他激动地走到她面前，开口说道：

“我挣扎过了，都是徒劳。再也撑不下去了。我无法压抑自己的感情。你必须允许我告诉你，我是多么热烈地仰慕你、爱恋你。”

伊丽莎白惊诧得无以复加。她瞪着眼，红了脸，满腹狐疑，默不作声。他将这视为足够的鼓励，随即倾吐了所有积压在心中已久的情感。他讲得很动听，但除了衷肠之外，还要诉说其他感受；他在谈及柔情时分，并不比在谈及傲慢时更富有口才。他对她出身低微、觉得这是自降身份、理智一直以家族障碍为由反对感情的这些想法，都用热烈的语气一一强调——那语气与其说是在推荐自己，不如说是在维护他即将伤害的自尊。

尽管她对他怀有根深蒂固的厌恶，却也无法对这样一个男子的爱慕之恭维无动于衷；虽然她的心意一刻也不曾动摇，起初仍不免为他即将承受的痛苦感到遗憾。直到他随后的话语激起了她的愤慨，她才在恼怒中完全丧失了怜悯。然而她还是尽力镇定自己，等他说完后耐心作答。他最后向她表明，这份情意他虽极力克制，却终究无法战胜；并表达了他的希望——如今这份情意能因她接受他的求婚而得到报偿。说到此处，她轻易看出他对答复胸有成竹。他口口声声说着担忧与焦虑，但神情流露的却是十足的自恃。这种情况只能令人更加恼怒；他话音一落，她双颊绯红，开口道：

“在这种情况下，我相信，通常的做法是，对表白出来的情意表示感激，无论这份情感回应得多么不相称。感到感激是很自然的，倘若我能生出感激之情，此刻就会向你道谢。但我不能——我从未希求得到你的好感，而你给予这份好感也极为勉强。让任何人感到痛苦，我都很抱歉。不过此事完全出于无心，但愿不会持续太久。你告诉我，这种感情长期阻碍你表明心迹，那么在今天这番解释之后，想来克服它也不会有多大难处。”

messages.content:
Translate the following technical explainer into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: preserve the technical meaning, use clear science-education language, and keep terms such as evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, groundwater recharge, and aquifer accurate. Do not add external information.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
水循环描述了水在地球上的分布及其运动方式。人类用水、土地利用和气候变化都在影响水循环。通过理解这些影响，我们能够朝着可持续用水的方向努力。

从太空俯瞰，我们星球最引人注目的特征便是水。无论是液态还是固态，水覆盖了地球表面75%的面积，并在天空中形成云层。水几乎遍布地球各处，从地壳内部到人体细胞中。需要牢记的是，所有这些水都在我们的星球上持续运动。

水储存于大气层、地表和地下。它可以呈现液态、固态或气态。液态水可为淡水或咸水。水在储存地点之间移动。水的运动覆盖大尺度范围，穿越流域、大气层和地壳深处；同时也存在于微观尺度，流淌于我们体内、植物及其他生物体中。

海洋储存了地球全部水资源的96%。海水是咸水，即含盐的水。陆地咸水储存在咸水湖中。地球上其余的水为淡水。淡水以液态形式储存在淡水湖、人工水库、河流和湿地中。水以固态形式储存在冰盖、冰川以及高海拔或地球极地附近的积雪中。

在运动过程中，水可以在液态、固态和气态之间转换。洋流混合海水并在大气中输送水蒸气。水通过蒸发、蒸散发和降水在大气与地表之间迁移。水通过融雪、径流和河流流动在地表移动。水通过入渗和地下水补给进入地下。地下水在地下的含水层中流动。地下水可通过自然排泄进入河流、海洋，或从泉水中返回地表。

水的运动既有自然过程，也受人类活动影响。太阳能和重力驱动着水在不同储库之间持续运动。太阳能量使液态水蒸发为水蒸气。蒸散发是水从地表和海洋进入大气的主要途径。重力使水在陆地上向下流动，并导致雨、雪和冰雹从云层中降落。

人类和其他生物依赖水维持生命。可用水资源的数量取决于每个储库中的水量、水在水循环中运动的时间与速度，以及水的洁净程度。通过理解人类对水循环的影响，我们能够朝着可持续用水的方向努力。

messages.content:
Write a single-file Python 3 command-line program named `schedule_optimizer.py`. Use no third-party packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Problem: Given a list of candidate work sessions, choose a non-overlapping subset that maximizes total value. Each session has an ID, start time, end time, and value. This is weighted interval scheduling with reconstruction.

Input: read JSON from standard input. Format:
{
  "sessions": [
    {"id": "a", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:30:00", "value": 5}
  ]
}

Requirements:
1. Parse ISO-like timestamps without time zones using Python standard library.
2. Reject sessions whose end is not after start or whose value is negative.
3. Output JSON with `max_value`, `selected_ids` in chronological order, and `rejected` entries with reasons.
4. If multiple optimal solutions exist, choose the one with fewer sessions; if still tied, choose lexicographically smaller list of IDs.
5. Include an efficient O(n log n) dynamic programming solution.
6. Include built-in tests. When run with `--test`, execute tests and print a short success message. Normal mode should print only JSON.

Output only the complete Python code. Do not include explanations.

Write a single-file Node.js HTTP server named `server.js`. Use only built-in Node.js modules; no Express, no npm packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Task: implement a small JSON API for a help-desk ticket queue stored in a local JSON file.

Data model:
- Ticket: `id`, `subject`, `description`, `priority` (`low`, `medium`, `high`), `status` (`open`, `in_progress`, `resolved`), `created_at`, `updated_at`.

API requirements:
1. `GET /tickets` with optional query params `status` and `priority`.
2. `POST /tickets` to create a ticket. Validate required fields.
3. `GET /tickets/{id}` to fetch one ticket.
4. `PATCH /tickets/{id}` to update `subject`, `description`, `priority`, or `status`.
5. `DELETE /tickets/{id}` to delete a ticket.
6. `GET /metrics` returning counts by status and priority plus average age of open tickets in seconds.

Engineering requirements:
- Read/write a `tickets.json` file safely enough for sequential requests.
- Return JSON for all responses, including errors.
- Include CORS headers for local browser testing.
- Handle invalid JSON, unknown routes, unsupported methods, and missing IDs.
- Start on `127.0.0.1:8080` by default.

Output only the complete JavaScript code. Do not include explanations.

Create a single-file frontend app named `index.html`. Do not use external libraries, CDNs, images, or build tools. Keep the total code under 1,000 lines.

Task: build an accessible “meeting notes board” for a small team.

Functional requirements:
1. Users can add a note with title, owner, meeting date, tags, and body.
2. Notes appear as cards and can be filtered by owner, tag, and free-text search.
3. A note can be marked as “Action Required,” “Decision,” or “Reference.”
4. Users can edit and delete notes.
5. Persist all data in `localStorage`.
6. Provide a compact statistics strip: total notes, action-required count, unique owners, and most common tag.
7. Responsive layout: two-column card grid on wide screens, single column on narrow screens.
8. Basic accessibility: labels for inputs, keyboard-operable controls, visible focus states.

Output only the complete `index.html` content, including HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Do not include explanatory text.

Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary for modern business readers.

Output requirements:
1. A 100-word executive summary.
2. Five principles of effective business writing implied by the author.
3. A table with two columns: “Original-era advice” and “Modern equivalent.” Include at least five rows.
4. Identify any advice that may be dated and explain how to update it without losing the underlying principle.
5. Do not add claims that are not grounded in the passage.

素材来源：The Book of Business Etiquette, chapter “The Business of Writing” longer excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Half the business letters which are written should never be written at all, and of the other half so many are incomplete or incoherent that a transaction which could be finished and filed away in two letters frequently requires six or eight.

A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around. Nothing but experience in copying her own notes will teach a stenographer to estimate them correctly so that she will not have to rewrite badly placed letters. It is a little point, but an important one.

Each subject considered in a letter should be treated in a separate paragraph, and each paragraph should be set off from the others by a wider space than that between the lines, double space between the paragraphs when there is single space between the lines, triple space between the paragraphs when there is a double space between the lines, and so on.

A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered. This enables the house receiving the letter to file it so that it can be found when it is needed.

When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin "Dear Mr. Brown" or "My dear Mr. Brown" than "Dear Sir" or "My dear Sir." "Gentlemen" or "Ladies" is sometime used in salutation when a letter is addressed to a group. "Dear Friend" is permissible in general letters sent out to persons of both sexes. Honorary titles should be used in the address when they take the place of "Mr.," such titles as Reverend, Doctor, Honorable (abbreviated to Rev., Dr., Hon.,) and the like. Titles should not be dropped except in the case of personal letters.

Special care should be taken with the outside address. State abbreviations should be used sparingly when there is a chance of confusion as in the case of Ga., Va., La., and Pa. "City" is not sufficient and should never be used. Nor should the name of the state ever be omitted even when the letter is addressed to some other point in the same state, as from New York to Brooklyn. And postage should be complete. A letter on which there is two cents due has placed itself under a pretty severe handicap before it is opened.

It is astonishing how many letters go out every day unsigned, lacking enclosures, carrying the wrong addresses, bearing insufficient postage, and showing other evidences of carelessness and thoughtlessness. In a town in New England last year one of the specialty shops received at Christmas time twenty different lots of money--money orders, stamps, and cash--by mail, not one of which bore the slightest clue to the identity of the sender. Countless times during the year this happens in every mail order house.

message.content:
Here is a structured English summary of the provided passage, tailored for modern business readers.

### Executive Summary (100 words)

Half of all business letters shouldn't be written; many more are so incomplete they require six to eight exchanges instead of two. Effective business writing stems from clear thinking and planning. The primary goals are to convey the idea clearly and build goodwill. Avoid flashy design that distracts from your message; substance, not style, sells. Be concise, courteous, and complete. Use standard formats on high-quality paper. Keep to one subject per letter, and address individuals by name. Always double-check for errors like missing signatures or incomplete postage. Carelessness undermines credibility before the message is even read.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing

1.  **Plan Before You Write:** A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning, not just typing.
2.  **Prioritize Clarity and Goodwill:** The only essential rules are to get the idea across successfully and to build goodwill for the firm.
3.  **Let the Message Lead:** Never write anything that calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver.
4.  **Standardize for Efficiency:** Use conventional formats and standard sizes to avoid confusion, look professional, and save time for everyone.
5.  **Be Complete and Accurate:** Check for errors like missing signatures, enclosures, or correct addresses before sending.

### Table: Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-Era Advice | Modern Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Use plain white paper; gaudy colors and ornate letterheads are taboo. | Use a clean, professional email signature or letterhead; avoid distracting fonts, backgrounds, or excessive graphics. |
| Use window envelopes with clear windows and opaque paper to conceal contents. | Use professional email formatting; ensure subject lines are clear and the body text is self-contained, protecting private information with BCC or secure file sharing. |
| Business letters should be typewritten, not handwritten, for professionalism. | Business communications should be typed (email, document) rather than handwritten (except for formal notes); use a spell-checker. |
| Handle only one subject per letter to enable easy filing. | Use a single, clear subject per email thread to enable easy searching and archiving. |
| Address an individual as "Dear Mr. Brown" rather than "Dear Sir." | Use a personalized salutation (e.g., "Hi Sarah," or "Dear Dr. Chen") in emails rather than "To Whom It May Concern." |

### Dated Advice & Modern Update

**Dated Advice:** "In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose... it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws."

**Explanation:** This reflects outdated gender roles and a hierarchical workflow (dictation vs. typing). The underlying principle is that the author of a message is ultimately responsible for its quality and appearance.

**Modern Update (Preserving the Principle):** **The person who drafts a communication is responsible for its final quality.** In modern teams, this means the writer (whether executive, manager, or individual contributor) should know how to format an email professionally, use a clear subject line, check for visual breaks in the text, and verify that any attachments or links are correct. While support staff may exist, the owner of the message cannot delegate responsibility for its professional presentation.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
When orphaned Mary Lennox is sent to Misselthwaite Manor, she is described as sour, thin, and yellow-faced. Born in India, she was unwanted by her beauty-obsessed mother and raised by servants who obeyed her every whim, making her tyrannical and selfish. One hot morning, her Ayah is replaced by a frightened stranger. Mary throws a tantrum, kicking the woman. Later, she overhears her mother speaking with a young officer about a terrible outbreak. Cholera has struck the household; servants are dying and fleeing. Mary hides in the nursery, forgotten by everyone. She eats leftover food and drinks wine, becoming drowsy, then falls asleep alone as chaos swells around her.

### Mary’s Character Traits (with concrete details)
- **Disagreeable-looking**: She has a “little thin face,” “sour expression,” and yellow hair and skin.
- **Tyrannical and selfish**: By age six, she is called “a tyrannical and selfish little pig as ever lived.”
- **Violent-tempered**: She “threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked” the frightened servant woman.
- **Insulting and rude**: She mutters, “Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” as the worst insult to the absent Ayah.
- **Lonely and unnoticed**: During the cholera chaos, “nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her,” and she hides alone.

### How the Setting Creates Emotional Distance or Isolation
The story opens in a hot, mysterious Indian bungalow during a cholera outbreak. Mary’s mother deliberately kept her out of sight, so she grew up surrounded only by servants who feared her. This physical and emotional distance deepens when the epidemic strikes: servants slink away with “ashy and scared faces,” and Mary is “forgotten by everyone.” She hides alone in her nursery, hearing wails and frantic footsteps but understanding nothing. The setting—a house in panic where no adult cares for or even remembers her—intensifies her isolation, leaving her utterly disconnected from the human tragedy unfolding around her.

### Three Discussion Questions (ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary reacts with anger and violence when the strange servant comes instead of her Ayah?
2. How does Mary’s mother treat her differently from the way most parents treat their children?
3. What clues in the passage show that Mary is completely alone during the cholera outbreak?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### 1. Concise 100-Word Overview
The water cycle describes the continuous movement and storage of water on Earth across the atmosphere, land surface, and underground. Water exists as liquid, solid, or gas and can be fresh or saline. Oceans hold 96% of Earth’s water. Movement is driven by solar energy and gravity, involving processes like evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. Water also moves through organisms. Human actions affect water movement, storage, and availability. Understanding these impacts supports sustainable water use.

### 2. Concept Map (Text Form)
```
Atmosphere (water vapor, clouds)
    ↑ Evaporation / Evapotranspiration      ↓ Precipitation (rain, snow, hail)
    ↓ Infiltration / Groundwater recharge
Land Surface (lakes, rivers, reservoirs, snowpack, ice sheets, glaciers)
    ↓ Runoff / Streamflow / Snowmelt
Groundwater (aquifers)
    ↓ Natural discharge (to rivers, ocean, springs)
Oceans (saline, 96% of all water)
    ↑ Evaporation / Evapotranspiration
```

### 3. Key Terms and Definitions
- **Water cycle**: The continuous movement and storage of water across Earth’s atmosphere, land, and underground.
- **Evaporation**: The process where solar energy turns liquid water into water vapor.
- **Evapotranspiration**: The main way water moves from land and oceans into the atmosphere, including plant transpiration.
- **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail falling from clouds to the surface, driven by gravity.
- **Infiltration**: The movement of water from the land surface into the ground.
- **Groundwater recharge**: The process by which water enters underground aquifers.
- **Aquifer**: An underground layer where groundwater flows and is stored.
- **Runoff**: Water that flows across the land surface toward streams, rivers, or oceans.

### 4. Human Influence
Human actions affect the water cycle by altering water use and land use. Withdrawing water for human use changes how much water remains in storage pools (e.g., lakes, rivers, groundwater). Land-use practices modify surface runoff, infiltration, and evapotranspiration rates. These activities influence the timing and speed of water movement and the availability of clean water for humans and other organisms. Understanding these impacts helps guide sustainable water management.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Warehouse system migration – update, impact, and next steps  

**To:** All Team Members  
**From:** Sarah Chen, Chief Operating Officer  
**Date:** March 28, 2025  

---  

Hi everyone,  

I want to give you a direct, honest update on last week’s warehouse management system (WMS) migration and the impact it’s had on our shipping speed and customer support teams.  

On March 20, we completed a planned migration to our new warehouse management platform – a project our operations and IT teams have been preparing for over six months. The migration itself executed on schedule, but once live, we encountered a series of data synchronization delays between the new WMS and our carrier booking systems. These delays cascaded into slower pick-pack cycles and, ultimately, a backlog of orders that took longer to ship than our usual 24-hour turnaround.  

**What happened – and why it didn’t go as planned**  
To be clear: this is not a failure of a single team, a vendor, or a piece of software. The root cause was an unanticipated latency in how the new system reconciles inventory availability with real-time carrier label generation. When orders queued faster than the system could fetch label data, pickers paused, and the queue grew. Our pre-launch testing did not replicate that volume and sequencing condition. We operated on the best available data, and we missed this pattern.  

**Current customer impact**  
As of this morning, shipping speed for orders placed between March 20 and March 27 has averaged 2.8 days from order to outbound scan, compared to our standard 1.0 days. Roughly 15% of orders in that window remain unshipped. Customer support volume has increased approximately 40% compared to the prior week, with the majority of inquiries asking “Where is my order?” or “Why hasn’t it shipped?” Response times for email and chat have stretched from an average of 4 hours to 14 hours.  

This is not acceptable, and I own that.  

**What we are doing today**  
- **Operations:** We have a temporary system patch deployed this morning that reduces the carrier label sync interval from once every 15 minutes to every 3 minutes. Early indicators show the pick-pack queue is starting to clear.  
- **Fulfillment centers:** Overtime is authorized for our warehouse teams today and tomorrow. I want to thank the warehouse supervisors and associates who have been working extended shifts under pressure – your focus and communication have been critical.  
- **Customer support:** We have added 10 temporary support agents from our overflow partner, and we are rerouting non-urgent email backlog to a dedicated triage queue so that live chat agents can focus on customers waiting for real-time answers.  
- **Cross-functional stand-up:** I am hosting a daily 30-minute stand-up at 9:00 AM PT with representatives from Operations, IT, Customer Support, and Logistics. Any blocker that cannot be resolved within two hours is escalated to me directly.  

**What will change next week**  
- **Monday:** A permanent fix for the carrier label latency will be deployed by our engineering and carrier integration teams. If that fix passes internal validation, we expect shipping speed to return to 1.0–1.5 days by Tuesday.  
- **Tuesday:** We will begin proactively emailing every customer whose order was delayed by more than 24 hours, offering a $10 credit or free expedited shipping on their next order. This communication will be plain and honest – no scripted apology, just what happened and what we’ve fixed.  
- **Wednesday:** A post-mortem will be published internally (not externally) detailing the exact technical trigger and the change we made to prevent recurrence. That document will be open for comment from any team member.  

**How to escalate urgent issues this week**  
If you are on the front line – in a warehouse, on a support call, or in a store – and you encounter a situation that standard procedures cannot resolve, please escalate directly to:  

- **Warehouse / fulfillment issues:** Operations director Alex Moreno (alex.moreno@company.com) – or page him via Slack in #ops-war-room.  
- **Customer support / order status issues:** Support director Nina Park (nina.park@company.com) – or tag her in #cx-escalations.  
- **Any technical blocker:** IT director Raj Patel (raj.patel@company.com) – or open a P1 ticket in Jira with “WMS-MIGRATION-ESC” in the title.  

If you escalate and do not receive a response within one hour, contact me directly at s.chen@company.com or via my cell (extension 511 in the directory). I mean that literally – I will respond.  

**Manager talking points**  

If you are a team lead, supervisor, or manager, please share the following concise summary with your team during huddles or one-on-ones this week:  

1. **What happened:** The WMS migration had a data sync delay that slowed shipping by about 1.8 days on average. This was not caused by any single team or person – it was an edge case our testing didn’t catch.  
2. **Current state:** Backlog is clearing. We have a temporary fix running today, and a permanent fix is expected Monday. Customer support volume is high but manageable with added staffing.  
3. **What we’re doing for customers:** Proactive email outreach with a credit will go out next week. We are not over-promising – we are telling customers the truth about the delay and what we fixed.  
4. **How to escalate:** Use the three contacts above for operations, support, or technical issues. If no response in one hour, escalate to Sarah.  

I know this has been a hard week for everyone, especially our warehouse associates and support team members who face frustrated customers directly. You have handled this with professionalism and patience that I deeply respect. My job this week is to remove every obstacle in your way and to be completely transparent about what went wrong.  

Thank you for the work you do every day to get our products into people’s homes.  

Best,  
Sarah Chen  
Chief Operating Officer

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
The Map of Puddles and the Sparrow Who Forgot to Land

The rain had stopped. Not the loud, crashy kind of rain, but the soft, sighing kind that leaves the garden smelling like wet dirt and green things. Every leaf wore a diamond. Every blade of grass dripped with a tiny silver tear.

At the bottom of the garden wall, near the crooked stone, a snail named Silas was making his map. Silas was the most careful snail in the whole garden. He did not rush. He did not slip. And he never, ever forgot a puddle.

His map was a curled, silvery trail on a smooth flat stone. Each loop meant a puddle. A big loop for the deep one under the rose bush. A tiny squiggle for the little one in the moss patch. He had mapped every puddle in the garden after every rain for three summers.

“Puddle under the wobbly fence post,” he murmured, writing with his foot. “Deep, but safe. Puddle by the parsley pot—shallow, good for a sip. And the Grand Puddle.” He paused. The Grand Puddle was the biggest of all, right in the middle of the sunny path. It was wide as six snail-shells and so clear you could see the sky swimming in it.

Just then, something landed with a *thump* on the grass beside him. It was a sparrow. But not an ordinary sparrow. This one was young, fluffier than a dandelion, with a yellow beak that opened before she even spoke.

“Hello, slow-circle!” she chirped. “I’m Pip. I’m going to fly all the way to that tall tree. Watch me!”

Silas’s eye-stalks wiggled. “That’s the oak at the top of the hill. It’s quite far.”

“Far is for birds with slow feet!” Pip fluffed her chest. She spread her wings, jumped, and soared. Up, up, past the parsley pot, over the wobbly fence post. She was fast, and she was high, and she was wonderful.

Then she turned to come down.

And that’s when the trouble started.

Pip aimed for the Grand Puddle—it looked like a perfect, soft landing spot. She tucked her wings and fell.

*SPLAT!*

She hit the water like a wet sock. Water flew everywhere. Pip spluttered and flapped, soaked and dizzy. She climbed out, shaking her head.

“I meant to do that,” she said, though her feathers were drooping.

Silas smiled. “You forgot to land. You remembered to fly, but landing is a different skill.”

Pip hopped from foot to foot. “Landing is boring! Flying is the brave part.”

Silas did not argue. He just pointed his two eye-stalks toward the Grand Puddle. “See how the water is shining? That puddle is my friend. After every rain, I map where it sits. But I also know something you don’t.”

“What?” Pip asked, shaking a drip off her beak.

“It won’t be here tomorrow.”

Pip stared at him. “Puddles stay forever, don’t they?”

“No,” said Silas. “The sun drinks them. Little by little, drop by drop, the water climbs into the sky and becomes a cloud. It’s called evaporation. It’s very slow. It takes all day.”

Pip looked at the puddle. “So… the puddle is disappearing right now?”

“Yes,” Silas whispered. “Every moment, it’s a tiny bit smaller. By night, it might be gone. But you have to watch very carefully to see it happen.”

Pip sat down, her wet feathers making a little puddle of their own. “That’s so slow. Why would anyone watch that?”

“Because,” Silas said, “the puddle doesn’t rush. It just lets the sun take its time. It is patient. And patience, little sparrow, can be a kind of bravery.”

Pip tilted her head. She had never heard that before. Bravery meant speeding toward the sky. It meant flapping hard. It did not mean sitting still.

But she was a curious sparrow. So she stayed.

She watched the edge of the puddle. The water was so clear she could see a tiny pebble at the bottom. She watched it for a long, quiet time. The pebble did not move. But slowly, so slowly she almost didn’t see it, the water line crept away from the pebble. The puddle was shrinking.

“It’s going,” she whispered.

“Yes,” said Silas. “Landing is like that. You have to be patient with your wings. You have to feel the air, wait for the wind, and set yourself down gently. It’s not as fast as flying. But it’s the bravest part, because you have to slow down.”

Pip looked at the oak tree. It was still far. But suddenly, she didn’t need to reach it today.

She hopped onto a low branch of the bush beside the Grand Puddle. “I’ll practice right here,” she said.

She jumped. She flapped. She tried to land on the same branch. The first time, she tumbled into the leaves. The second time, she grabbed it with her feet but swung upside down. The third time, she wobbled and almost fell.

Silas did not laugh. He just watched the puddle.

On the fourth try, Pip spread her wings wide, slowed down, and touched the branch with both feet. She swayed, but she stayed.

“I did it!” she chirped softly.

Silas smiled. “You were patient. That was brave.”

The sun moved across the garden. The Grand Puddle shrank to a saucer, then to a cup, then to a wet shadow on the stone. By the time the evening breeze arrived, it was gone.

But Pip did not mourn it. She was on the low branch, then the middle branch, then the top of the bush. She was learning to land. And every time she came down, she thought of the patient puddle, letting the sun drink it, drop by drop, until it was part of a cloud.

“Tomorrow,” she said to Silas, “I’ll learn to land on the fence.”

“And the day after,” Silas said, “the parsley pot.”

“And one day,” Pip said, her voice full of wonder, “the oak tree. But I’ll get there slowly.”

Silas tucked himself into his shell for the night. His map on the stone was already faint with dew. But he knew tomorrow, after the next rain, he would make a new one. And maybe a little sparrow would sit beside him and watch.

Because patience, he knew, wasn’t slow after all.

It was just a different way of flying.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Opens New “Pollinator Path” to Educate Public on Habitat Creation**

**HARBORLEAF, PA – July 1, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden will officially open its new “Pollinator Path” public education trail on July 12, 2026, offering visitors a hands-on, accessible resource for learning how to support bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds through native plant choices. The trail, located along the east meadow loop, responds directly to visitor requests for practical, actionable guidance on creating pollinator-friendly habitats at home.

The 0.8-mile accessible trail winds through the Garden’s east meadow, featuring a series of demonstration beds planted exclusively with native species that provide nectar, pollen, and larval host plants for regional pollinators. Key stops along the route include a night-blooming flower station, designed to highlight plants that support moths and other crepuscular pollinators, and tactile signage with raised text and botanical illustrations for visitors with visual impairments. Youth observation cards, available at the trailhead, encourage children to document the insects and flowers they encounter, while shaded resting points offer respite and opportunities for reflection.

The Pollinator Path will be open daily during regular Garden hours. In addition to self-guided exploration, the Garden will host volunteer-led weekend walks every Saturday and Sunday at 10:00 a.m. beginning July 12. These walks, led by trained volunteer docents, will provide deeper insights into the ecological roles of specific plants and the life cycles of common pollinators.

Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden, emphasized that the trail is designed to bridge the gap between general awareness of pollinator decline and concrete, actionable steps that individuals can take. “We kept hearing from visitors who said, ‘I know I should help pollinators, but I don’t know where to start,’” Ortiz said. “The Pollinator Path gives them a model. It shows that small planting decisions—like choosing a single milkweed over an ornamental grass, or letting a patch of clover bloom—can create connected habitats that run across neighborhoods, from one garden to the next. That cumulative effect is powerful.”

The Garden developed the trail based on feedback collected during visitor surveys and program evaluations conducted over the past two years. Many respondents expressed interest in learning about plants that support specific pollinator species, as well as how to select native alternatives for common ornamental plants. The demonstration beds include species such as butterfly weed, wild bergamot, purple coneflower, and evening primrose, all of which are native to the region and widely adaptable to home gardens.

The night-blooming flower station, a feature unique to the Pollinator Path, will showcase plants such as moonflower, night-blooming jasmine, and evening stock, which open their flowers in the late afternoon and evening to attract moths. Tactile signage at this station includes raised diagrams of moth antennae and flower shapes, providing an inclusive educational experience for visitors with low vision. Youth observation cards, printed on recycled paper, prompt children to identify flower colors, insect visitors, and weather conditions, with space for drawings and notes.

The trail surface is constructed with compacted, permeable material to accommodate wheelchairs, strollers, and mobility devices, and the gradient remains gentle throughout the loop. Benches made from reclaimed wood are positioned at intervals, shaded by existing tree canopy. The Garden has also installed small interpretive panels in braille and large-print text at each station, ensuring that non-sighted and low-vision visitors can access the content independently.

Volunteer-led weekend walks will rotate through different themes each month, including “Moon Moth Monday” orientations at the night-blooming station, “Bee Basics” focusing on solitary and native bee identification, and “Butterfly Life Cycles” highlighting host plants. The walks are free with Garden admission, and no advance registration is required. Groups of ten or more are encouraged to contact the Garden to schedule a private tour.

The Pollinator Path is the most recent addition to Harborleaf Botanical Garden’s public programming, which includes school field trips, adult workshops, and community gardening initiatives. The Garden, which spans 120 acres in eastern Pennsylvania, maintains collections of native plants, woody ornamentals, and heritage gardens.

“We are not advocating for any single approach to gardening,” Ortiz said. “We are simply showing that when you plant with intention—when you choose native flowers and trees that evolved alongside local insects—you create a stepping stone. That stone connects to the next garden, and the next, forming a web of life that benefits both wildlife and people. The Pollinator Path is a teaching tool for that idea.”

The Garden will host a brief ribbon-cutting ceremony at the east meadow trailhead on July 12 at 9:30 a.m., followed by the first volunteer-led weekend walk at 10:00 a.m. The event is open to the public. No elected officials, celebrities, or corporate partners have been scheduled to speak, and funding for the trail has come from the Garden’s existing operating budget.

**About Harborleaf Botanical Garden**
Harborleaf Botanical Garden is a nonprofit public garden dedicated to horticultural education, plant conservation, and community engagement. Located in Harborleaf, Pennsylvania, the Garden offers year-round programming, plant collections, and natural areas for exploration. More information is available at www.harborleafgarden.org.

# # #

**Media Contact:**
Alice Chen
Communications Manager
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
(555) 123-4567
achen@harborleafgarden.org

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The laundromat on Harbor Street smelled of bleach, salt, and damp cotton. The power had gone out twenty minutes ago, plunging the small coastal town into a silence that felt heavier than the storm that caused it. Through the plate-glass windows, the streetlights were dead, and the surf beyond the dunes was a low, constant exhale in the dark.

Lena had three loads to fold and a ferry to catch in the morning. She was bent over a stainless-steel table, squinting at the dim shape of a towel, when the door creaked open. A rectangle of gray twilight widened, then narrowed as someone stepped inside. She didn’t look up. The outage had drawn a few other stragglers from the beach rentals—a man with a sandy dog, a woman holding a baby. This was just another soul with damp clothes and a bad sense of timing.

Then he said her name.

Not loudly. Almost a question. “Lena?”

She straightened. The towel slipped from her fingers and landed in a heap on the table. The man stood just inside the doorway, one hand still on the handle, the other holding a battery lantern. The light cast his face in sharp, shadowed planes: the straight nose, the slight hollow beneath his cheekbone. She had not seen that face for seven years.

“Leo,” she said. Her own voice sounded strange to her, like a word she was tasting for the first time.

He set the lantern on the nearest folding counter. Its glow pushed the darkness back only a few feet, pooling around his boots and the edge of a washing machine. He wore a canvas jacket, salt-stained, and his dark hair was longer than she remembered, curling at the collar. He looked older, not in a bad way, but in a way that suggested he had been living his life in real time, without her.

“I thought that was you,” he said. “The way you fold a towel. Corner to corner, then thirds.” He gestured at the pile. “You always did that.”

She had forgotten he noticed that. She had forgotten he noticed so many small things. “It’s faster,” she said. “Less wrinkling.”

A pause. The wind rattled the doorframe. Outside, the storm was moving inland, dragging the last of the rain with it. The air inside was cooling quickly.

“You still live here?” he asked.

“No. Just visiting. Old rental. My aunt’s place.” She gestured vaguely toward the window. “You?”

“Yeah. I moved back two years ago. After my dad passed.” He said it flatly, like a fact he had already accepted. Then he looked at her, and something in his eyes tightened. “You left kind of quick.”

The kindness of the word quick. She had left in three hours. Packed a suitcase, dropped her keys on the counter, and driven inland before the sun was up. She had not looked back. She had not said goodbye.

“I know,” she said. It was all she could manage.

The lantern hummed faintly. A dryer in the far corner clicked, then stopped as the power flickered and died again, leaving only the yellow bubble of light around them.

“I left you a message,” he said. “The morning you left. I didn’t know you were going, I just… I wanted to hear your voice. So I called. You didn’t answer.” He said it without accusation, but she felt the weight of it settle between them like a stone.

“I never listened to it,” she said. “I was afraid to.”

He let out a breath that might have been a laugh. “I was afraid to leave it.” He pulled his hands from his pockets and rubbed them together, not from cold, but from the nervous habit she remembered. He used to do that when he was trying to say something important. “I said a lot of stupid things in that message. Probably for the best you didn’t hear it.”

“I still have it,” she said.

He looked up.

“The voicemail.” She felt her face grow warm. “I never deleted it. I changed phones three times, and I always transferred the files. I don’t know why. I just… couldn’t let it go.”

The laundromat was silent except for the faint drip of a faucet. The lantern threw their shadows long and distorted against the tile wall. He was looking at her now, not with anger, but with the kind of attention that made her feel seen in a way she had tried very hard to avoid for seven years.

“I should have said goodbye,” she said. The words came out rough, unpracticed. “I was twenty-two. I thought if I stayed one more minute I would never leave. And I was scared of how much I didn’t want to go.”

He nodded slowly. “I think I knew that. Even then.” He picked up the lantern and moved it to the table where her towels lay unfolded. The light fell across the damp fabric, illuminating the faint blue stripe along the hem. “I used to wonder if you thought about me. After.”

“I did,” she said. “I do.”

It was the most honest thing she had said in years. It hung in the air, delicate, breakable. She waited for him to say something that would shatter it, but he only reached out and touched the edge of the towel she had let fall. He smoothed it, corner to corner, then folded it into a neat square. His hands were steady.

“You want help with these?” he asked.

She almost laughed. “There’s no power. They’ll take hours to dry.”

“So we’ll wait.” He set the folded towel on the pile. “Unless you have somewhere to be.”

She didn’t. The ferry wasn’t until morning. The rental was empty and cold. She had nowhere to be except here, in a laundromat with no electricity and a man she had left without a word.

She picked up a towel and began to fold. Corner to corner, then thirds. He did the same, matching her rhythm. They worked in silence for a while, the only sound the soft whisper of cotton and the distant roll of the sea.

After a few minutes, he spoke again, his voice low. “I don’t need an apology, Lena. I just needed to know you were okay.”

She stopped folding. The towel hung from her hands like a question. “I’m okay,” she said. It was nearly true.

He looked at her, and in the lantern light, his face was unreadable—not guarded, but patient. He was not going to push. He was not going to ask for more than she could give. After seven years, he was still the same generous presence she had been too young to hold on to.

She set down the towel. “Thank you,” she said, though she wasn’t sure what she was thanking him for. The message? The patience? The simple act of standing in a dark room with her, folding towels as if no time had passed?

He nodded. “You’re welcome.”

The power did not return. The storm moved on, leaving the sky clear and the air rinsed clean. Through the window, the first stars appeared, faint and distant.

When the last towel was folded, Lena picked up her bag. Leo lifted the lantern. They stood at the door, neither moving to open it.

“Goodnight, Leo,” she said.

“Goodnight, Lena.”

She stepped out into the dark. The lantern light followed her for a few steps, then fell away as the door closed. She walked toward the ferry terminal, and behind her, the laundromat glowed faintly through the glass, the only lit window on the street.

She did not look back. But she knew he was watching.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# Bounded by Design: Rate Limiting for the Multi-Tenant API

## 1. The Four Pillars of Access Control

Before discussing rate limiting, we must distinguish it from three closely related concepts that often get conflated.

**Authentication** answers *who you are*. It’s the credential check—API key, OAuth token, mTLS certificate. Without it, no request proceeds. A rate limit is meaningless if unauthenticated traffic can exhaust it.

**Authorization** answers *what you can do*. It governs access to specific resources or actions (e.g., “tenant A can read, tenant B can write”). A properly authorized request still must pass rate limiting.

**Quotas** are *hard, often contractual limits* enforced over longer intervals (daily, monthly). Exceeding a quota means the tenant must upgrade their plan or wait for the next billing cycle. Quotas are typically checked asynchronously, not on every request.

**Rate limits** are *soft, short-term limits* (per second, per minute) that protect system stability. Exceeding a rate limit is transient—the client can retry after a brief cooldown. Rate limits are checked synchronously on every request.

The key insight: authentication and authorization gate **access to the API**; quotas and rate limits gate **access to capacity**. Both are needed, but they solve different problems.

## 2. Three Algorithms at a Practical Level

### Fixed Window
Divide time into discrete buckets (e.g., 60-second windows). Count requests per bucket. When a bucket fills, reject until the next bucket opens.

**Reality check:** This algorithm is simple to implement (a single counter per window, resetting on a timer) but suffers from *boundary spikes*. If a tenant sends 1,000 requests at the last millisecond of window N and 1,000 requests at the first millisecond of window N+1, the system sees 2,000 requests in near-zero time. Fixed windows thus require a safety margin—lowering the limit to absorb these bursts—which wastes capacity.

### Sliding Window Log
Track timestamps of every request (e.g., in a sorted set or circular buffer). On each request, remove timestamps older than the window duration, then count remaining timestamps.

**Reality check:** This removes the boundary spike problem but introduces memory and CPU overhead proportional to throughput at the window boundary. For a tenant doing 10,000 requests/minute, you must store and sort 10,000 timestamps. For multi-tenant systems with thousands of active tenants, this becomes a hot loop that needs careful data structure choice (e.g., a rolling buffer with O(1) eviction).

### Token Bucket
A bucket holds a fixed number of tokens (the *capacity*). Tokens are added at a fixed *refill rate* (e.g., 10 tokens per second). Each request consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, the request is rejected.

**Reality check:** Token bucket is the industry favorite for three reasons. First, it naturally allows bursts up to the bucket capacity—if a tenant hasn’t sent requests for 10 seconds, they can send 100 at once (if capacity=100). Second, it aligns with how TCP and connection pooling work: a client can send a burst right after connection establishment. Third, implementation is trivial: two atomic integers (tokens, last_refill_timestamp) and a refill calculation on each request. The only pitfall is clock skew in distributed systems—use monotonic time or a central coordinator.

**Practical recommendation:** Token bucket for per-tenant limits. Fixed window only if you need global, non-burstable limits (e.g., “no more than 1,000 logins per hour per IP”). Avoid sliding window logs unless you have a specific need for exact counting (e.g., enforcing strict compliance SLAs).

## 3. The Noisy Neighbor Problem

In multi-tenant systems, one tenant might have an integration that sends bursts (e.g., a webhook retry storm, a buggy client, or a sudden data sync). Without isolation, this tenant’s behavior can exhaust shared resources (database connections, CPU, network bandwidth) and degrade service for everyone.

**The cardinal rule: never rate-limit at the shared resource level.** Instead, institute hierarchical limits:

- **Global pool:** A soft cap on total system throughput. If crossed, begin rejecting *all tenants* as a last resort, but this should never happen in normal operation.
- **Per-tenant bucket:** Each tenant gets its own token bucket (capacity = their plan limit × burst multiplier). The key design choice: the per-tenant limit is *independent* of other tenants. Tenant A’s burst must not consume tokens from Tenant B’s bucket.
- **Per-integration sub-buckets (optional):** For enterprise tenants, allow them to create API keys scoped to specific integrations. Each key gets its own sub-bucket with capacity `(parent_tenant_capacity / N)`. This prevents one buggy webhook from exhausting the tenant’s entire limit.

**A critical nuance:** If a tenant hits their per-tenant limit, reject only *that* tenant’s requests. Do not degrade or delay other tenants. This requires your rate limiter to be a pure function that takes (tenant_id, request_timestamp) and returns (allow/deny) without global state that can be contaminated by other tenants’ counters.

**Warning against shared counters:** Avoid designs where all tenants share a single sliding window or a global token bucket. This turns every noisy neighbor into a global outage. Instead, use sharding by tenant_id across your rate limiter instances (e.g., hash tenant_id to a consistent backend that holds that tenant’s state in memory).

## 4. Headers and Error Messages That Help Clients

Clients need enough information to self-correct. Return the following headers on every response:

- `X-RateLimit-Limit`: The maximum requests allowed per window (or bucket size). Helps clients know their ceiling.
